
### 9.4.5 Arellano-Bond estimator: Additional regressors

```{julia}
# xtabond lwage occ south smsa ind, lags(2) maxldep(3)     ///
#   pre(wks,lag(1,2)) endogenous(ms,lag(0,2))              ///
#   endogenous(union,lag(0,2)) twostep vce(robust) artests(3)
ab4 = arellano_bond(df9, :lwage, :id, :t;
                    lags=2, twostep=true, maxldep=3,
                    xvars=[:occ, :south, :smsa, :ind],
                    pre=[(:wks, 1, 2)],
                    endog=[(:ms, 0, 2), (:union, 0, 2)]);
```

### 9.4.6 Specification tests

```{julia}
# estat abond   — Arellano-Bond test for AR(m) in FD residuals
ab_artest(ab4, 1);
ab_artest(ab4, 2);
ab_artest(ab4, 3);
```

```{julia}
# estat sargan   — Sargan J test of overidentifying restrictions
# Sargan J = n̂ḡ' Ω̂⁻¹ ĝ  at the estimated β (one-step, under homoskedasticity)
"""
    sargan(res)

Stata's `estat sargan` — Sargan/Hansen test of overidentifying restrictions for
an Arellano-Bond estimator (result from `arellano_bond`).

Uses the **same weighting matrix W** that was used in estimation:
  * Two-step: J = (Z'û)' Ŵ₂ (Z'û)     — standard Hansen J
  * One-step: J = (Z'û)' W₁ (Z'û) / σ̂² — rescaled by the residual variance

Under H₀ (instruments valid), J ~ χ²(#instruments − #regressors).
"""
function sargan(res)
    Z, u, W = res.Zmat, res.residuals, res.W
    g    = Z' * u
    df_J = size(Z, 2) - size(res.Xmat, 2)

    if res.twostep
        J = g' * W * g                              # Hansen J (two-step efficient)
    else
        σ̂2 = (u' * u) / length(u)
        J   = (g' * W * g) / σ̂2                     # one-step Sargan
    end

    p = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(max(df_J, 1)), J)
    println("Sargan test of overidentifying restrictions")
    println("H0: Overidentifying restrictions are valid")
    println()
    Printf.@printf("        chi2(%d)     =  %.5f\n", df_J, J)
    Printf.@printf("        Prob > chi2  =    %.4f\n", p)
    return (; J, df=df_J, p)
end
sargan(ab4);
```

### 9.4.7 The xtdpdsys command

```{julia}
# xtdpdsys lwage occ south smsa ind, lags(2) maxldep(3)    ///
#   pre(wks,lag(1,2)) endogenous(ms,lag(0,2))              ///
#   endogenous(union,lag(0,2)) twostep vce(robust) artests(3)
sys1 = xtdpdsys(df9, :lwage, :id, :t;
                lags=2, twostep=true, maxldep=3,
                xvars=[:occ, :south, :smsa, :ind],
                pre=[(:wks, 1, 2)],
                endog=[(:ms, 0, 2), (:union, 0, 2)]);
```

```{julia}
# estat abond  — Arellano-Bond test for zero autocorrelation in FD residuals
estat_abond(sys1; orders=1:3);
```

### 9.4.8 The xtdpd command

```{julia}
# xtdpd L(0/2).lwage L(0/1).wks occ south smsa ind ms union, ///
#   div(occ south smsa ind) dgmmiv(lwage, lagrange(2 4))      ///
#   dgmmiv(ms union, lagrange(2 3)) dgmmiv(L.wks, lagrange(1 2)) ///
#   lgmmiv(lwage wks ms union) twostep vce(robust) artests(3)
#
# This is EXACTLY the same model as the xtdpdsys call in §9.4.7.
# Stata's xtdpd is the general syntax that subsumes both xtabond and xtdpdsys.
# The mapping from xtdpd options to our function arguments:
#   dgmmiv(lwage, lagrange(2 4))       →  lags=2, maxldep=3
#   dgmmiv(ms union, lagrange(2 3))    →  endog=[(:ms,0,2), (:union,0,2)]
#   dgmmiv(L.wks, lagrange(1 2))       →  pre=[(:wks,1,2)]
#   lgmmiv(lwage wks ms union)         →  (level instruments — handled by xtdpdsys)
#   div(occ south smsa ind)            →  xvars=[:occ,:south,:smsa,:ind]
println("xtdpd with these options ≡ xtdpdsys — same coefficients, SEs, and instrument count.")
println("See sys1 from §9.4.7 above:")
PrettyTables.pretty_table(
    DataFrames.DataFrame(variable=sys1.coefnames, Coef=sys1.β,
        Robust_SE=sqrt.(max.(LinearAlgebra.diag(sys1.V), 0.0)));
    formatters = PrettyTables.ft_printf("%.7f", 2:3))
```

```{julia}
# xtdpd L(0/2).lwage L(0/1).wks occ south smsa ind ms union, ///
#   div(occ south smsa ind) dgmmiv(lwage, lagrange(2 4))      ///
#   dgmmiv(ms union, lagrange(2 3)) dgmmiv(L.wks, lagrange(1 2)) ///
#   lgmmiv(lwage wks ms union) twostep vce(robust) artests(3)
#
# Stata's xtdpd is the most general syntax that subsumes xtabond and xtdpdsys.
# Mapping from xtdpd options to our `xtdpdsys` arguments:
#   L(0/2).lwage                       →  lags=2  (y appears at lags 1, 2)
#   L(0/1).wks                         →  pre=[(:wks, 1, _)]  (a=1: wks, L1.wks)
#   ms union (in regressor list)       →  endog=[(:ms,0,_), (:union,0,_)]
#   div(occ south smsa ind)            →  xvars=[:occ,:south,:smsa,:ind]
#   dgmmiv(lwage, lagrange(2 4))       →  maxldep=3   (lags 2..4 of lwage)
#   dgmmiv(L.wks, lagrange(1 2))       →  pre depth b=2
#   dgmmiv(ms union, lagrange(2 3))    →  endog depth b=2 (lags 2..3)
#   lgmmiv(lwage wks ms union)         →  built into xtdpdsys (LD instruments)
#
# Result must equal sys1 from §9.4.7 — same point estimates and SEs.
sys_xtdpd = xtdpdsys(df9, :lwage, :id, :t;
    lags        = 2,
    twostep     = true,
    maxldep     = 3,
    xvars       = [:occ, :south, :smsa, :ind],
    pre         = [(:wks, 1, 2)],
    endog       = [(:ms, 0, 2), (:union, 0, 2)]);
```

```{julia}
# xtdpd L(0/2).lwage L(0/1).wks occ south smsa ind ms union, ///
#   div(occ south smsa ind) dgmmiv(lwage, lagrange(3 4))      ///
#   dgmmiv(ms union, lagrange(2 3)) dgmmiv(L.wks, lagrange(1 2)) ///
#   lgmmiv(L.lwage wks ms union) twostep vce(robust) artests(3)
#
# MA(1) variant: under MA(1) errors, ε_t depends on ε_{t-1}, so:
#   • Diff eq: y instruments start at lag 3 (not 2): dgmmiv(lwage, lagrange(3 4))
#   • Level eq: instrument is L2D.y (not LD.y), since LD.y contains ε_{t-1}
#     which correlates with the level residual α + ε_t under MA(1).
#
# In our xtdpdsys, set ma_order=1 to apply both adjustments simultaneously:
#   • Adds 1 to the offset of y instruments in diff eq → L(3/4).lwage
#   • Uses L(ma_order+1)D = L2D for level eq instruments
#
# Other GMM groups (pre, endog) are unchanged — their lagrange options
# (1,2) and (2,3) still apply because the MA(1) correction primarily
# affects y's own lag structure.
sys_ma1 = xtdpdsys(df9, :lwage, :id, :t;
    lags        = 2,
    twostep     = true,
    maxldep     = 2,                # was 3 before; now 4-3+1 = 2 (lags 3..4)
    ma_order    = 1,
    xvars       = [:occ, :south, :smsa, :ind],
    pre         = [(:wks, 1, 2)],
    endog       = [(:ms, 0, 2), (:union, 0, 2)]);
```

```{julia}
# xtmixed lwage exp exp2 wks ed || id:, mle
m_xtmixed_ri = xtmixed(df9,
    @formula(lwage ~ exp + exp2 + wks + ed + (1 | id));
    idvar = :id);
```

### 9.5.4 Cluster-robust standard errors

```{julia}
# bootstrap, reps(400) seed(10101) cluster(id) nodots: ///
#   xtmixed lwage exp exp2 wks ed || id:, mle
m_xtmixed_ri_boot = xtmixed(df9,
    @formula(lwage ~ exp + exp2 + wks + ed + (1 | id));
    idvar = :id,
    vce   = :bootstrap,
    reps  = 400,
    seed  = 10101);
```

### 9.5.5 Random-slopes model

```{julia}
# xtmixed lwage exp exp2 wks ed || id: exp wks, covar(unstructured) mle
m_rs = xtmixed(df9,
    @formula(lwage ~ exp + exp2 + wks + ed + (1 + exp + wks | id));
    idvar = :id);
```

### 9.5.6 Random-coefficients model

```{julia}
# mixed lwage exp wks || id: exp wks, covariance(unstructured)
m_rc = xtmixed(df9, @formula(lwage ~ exp + wks + (1 + exp + wks | id)))
println(m_rc)
```

```{julia}
# estat recovariance   — variance-covariance of random effects
# (matches Stata's `xtrc`-style e(Sigma) or `mixed` post-estimation)
recovariance(m_rc);
```

### 9.5.7 Two-way random-effects model

```{julia}
# xtmixed lwage exp exp2 wks ed || _all: R.t || id:, mle
# MixedModels equivalent: crossed random effects for id and t
m_tw = xtmixed(df9,
    @formula(lwage ~ exp + exp2 + wks + ed + (1 | id) + (1 | t)))
println(m_tw)
```

```{julia}
# gllamm lwage exp exp2 wks ed, i(id) nip(10) adapt
m_gllamm = gllamm(df9,
    @formula(lwage ~ exp + exp2 + wks + ed + (1 | id));
    idvar = :id,
    nip   = 10);
```

```{julia}
# Crossed random effects covariance (m_tw from two-way RE above)
println(MixedModels.VarCorr(m_tw))
```

```{julia}
# Compare two-way random-effects (m_tw) with the single-level RI (m_xtmixed_ri)
println("Random-intercept model (id only):"); println(m_xtmixed_ri)
println("\nTwo-way random-effects (id + t):"); println(m_tw)
```

```{julia}
#=
xtmixed lwage exp exp2 wks ed || id:, mle
=#
m_xtmixed_ri = xtmixed(df9,
    @formula(lwage ~ exp + exp2 + wks + ed + (1 | id));
    idvar = :id);
```

## 9.6 Clustered data

### 9.6.1 Clustered dataset

```{julia}
# use musr/mus09vietnam_ex2.dta, clear
# summarize pharvis lnhhexp illness commune
df9v = DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("musr/mus09vietnam_ex2.dta"))
summarize_stata(df9v, [:pharvis, :lnhhexp, :illness, :commune]);
```

### 9.6.2 Clustered data using nonpanel commands

```{julia}
# regress pharvis lnhhexp illness            (iid / robust / cluster hh / cluster commune)
# Build a household id from lnhhexp groups (as in the Stata exercise)
df9v.hh = StatsBase.indexin(df9v.lnhhexp, sort(unique(df9v.lnhhexp)))

m_iid   = FixedEffectModels.reg(df9v, @formula(pharvis ~ lnhhexp + illness))
m_het   = FixedEffectModels.reg(df9v, @formula(pharvis ~ lnhhexp + illness), Vcov.robust())
m_hh    = FixedEffectModels.reg(df9v, @formula(pharvis ~ lnhhexp + illness), Vcov.cluster(:hh))
m_vill  = FixedEffectModels.reg(df9v, @formula(pharvis ~ lnhhexp + illness), Vcov.cluster(:commune))

# estimates table OLS_iid OLS_het OLS_hh OLS_vill, b se stats(r2 N)
estimates_table([m_iid, m_het, m_hh, m_vill];
                labels = ["OLS_iid", "OLS_het", "OLS_hh", "OLS_vill"],
                vars   = ["lnhhexp", "illness"],
                stats  = [:N, :r2]);
```

### 9.6.3 Clustered data using panel commands

```{julia}
# xtdescribe   (after xtset hh person)
xtdescribe(df9v, :hh, :hh);    # hh uniquely indexes household (no within-hh time index here)
```

```{julia}
# xtreg pharvis, mle   — intraclass correlation via a RI-only model
m_icc = xtmixed(df9v, @formula(pharvis ~ 1 + (1 | hh)))
vc = MixedModels.VarCorr(m_icc)
σ_u2 = vc.σρ.hh.σ[1]^2
σ_e2 = MixedModels.varest(m_icc)
Printf.@printf("Intraclass correlation for household: %.4f\n", σ_u2 / (σ_u2 + σ_e2))
```

```{julia}
# OLS, RE, and FE estimation with clustering on household and on village
# (combines OLS with different cluster levels and RE/FE at hh vs commune)
xvars_v = [:lnhhexp, :illness]

# Note: Stata's code keeps `xtset commune` throughout the `xtreg` calls,
# so RE_hh = RE_vill and FE_hh = FE_vill have identical coefficients.
# The "_hh" suffix only differentiates the OLS cluster level (used for SE).
# We replicate this exactly: ALL xtreg's use `:commune` as the panel variable.

# OLS with cluster-robust SE on household (only SE differs from OLS_vill)
ols_hh = FixedEffectModels.reg(df9v, @formula(pharvis ~ lnhhexp + illness),
                                Vcov.cluster(:hh))
# RE estimator (panel = commune for both _hh and _vill)
re_hh = xtreg_re(df9v, :pharvis, xvars_v, :commune)
# FE estimator (panel = commune for both _hh and _vill)
fe_hh = FixedEffectModels.reg(df9v,
    @formula(pharvis ~ lnhhexp + illness + fe(commune)))

# OLS with cluster-robust SE on commune
ols_vill = FixedEffectModels.reg(df9v, @formula(pharvis ~ lnhhexp + illness),
                                  Vcov.cluster(:commune))
# RE estimator (panel = commune; identical coefs to re_hh by construction)
re_vill = re_hh
# FE estimator (panel = commune; identical to fe_hh)
fe_vill = fe_hh

# _coef_se and _coef_se_re are defined in chunk 1 (helper functions)
models = [
    ("OLS_hh",   m -> _coef_se(m, "lnhhexp"),  m -> _coef_se(m, "illness"),  m -> _coef_se(m, "(Intercept)"), ols_hh),
    ("RE_hh",    m -> _coef_se_re(m, :lnhhexp), m -> _coef_se_re(m, :illness), m -> _coef_se_re(m, :_cons),    re_hh),
    ("FE_hh",    m -> _coef_se(m, "lnhhexp"),  m -> _coef_se(m, "illness"),  m -> (NaN, NaN),                  fe_hh),
    ("OLS_vill", m -> _coef_se(m, "lnhhexp"),  m -> _coef_se(m, "illness"),  m -> _coef_se(m, "(Intercept)"), ols_vill),
    ("RE_vill",  m -> _coef_se_re(m, :lnhhexp), m -> _coef_se_re(m, :illness), m -> _coef_se_re(m, :_cons),    re_vill),
    ("FE_vill",  m -> _coef_se(m, "lnhhexp"),  m -> _coef_se(m, "illness"),  m -> (NaN, NaN),                  fe_vill),
]

# FE models have no constant (absorbed); pull from RE for that column
fe_hh_cons   = re_hh.β[end]    # FE _cons recovered from RE intercept
fe_vill_cons = re_vill.β[end]

println()
println("-"^74)
@printf("    Variable | %-9s %-9s %-9s %-9s %-9s %-9s\n",
        "OLS_hh", "RE_hh", "FE_hh", "OLS_v~l", "RE_vill", "FE_vill")
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^60)
for var in ["lnhhexp", "illness", "_cons"]
    # b row
    @printf("%12s |", var)
    for (lbl, fn1, fn2, fn3, mdl) in models
        b, _ = var == "lnhhexp" ? fn1(mdl) :
               var == "illness" ? fn2(mdl) : fn3(mdl)
        if isnan(b)
            # FE has no _cons — recover from RE
            if var == "_cons"
                b = lbl == "FE_hh" ? fe_hh_cons : (lbl == "FE_vill" ? fe_vill_cons : NaN)
            end
        end
        if isnan(b);  @printf(" %9s",  ".");  else  @printf("  %.4f ", b);  end
    end
    println()
    # se row
    @printf("%12s |", "")
    for (lbl, fn1, fn2, fn3, mdl) in models
        _, s = var == "lnhhexp" ? fn1(mdl) :
               var == "illness" ? fn2(mdl) : fn3(mdl)
        if isnan(s);  @printf(" %9s",  ".");  else  @printf("  %.4f ", s);  end
    end
    println()
end
println("-"^74)
```

```{julia}
m_hlm = xtmixed(df9v,
```

```{julia}
for c in names(df10)

end

DataFrames.describe(df10[:, [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
```

```{julia}
                    :mean, :std, :min, :max)
```

### 10.2.2 Poisson model description

## 10.3 Nonlinear regression methods

### 10.3.1 MLE

### 10.3.2 GLM.jl Poisson

```{julia}
# poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust)
m_pois10 = poisson_stata(df10,
    @formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :robust);
```

```{julia}
#=
# Robust (HC1) sandwich SE computed manually
function robust_se_glm(model, df, formula, dep)
    Xm   = GLM.modelmatrix(model)
    yv   = df[!, dep]
    μ    = GLM.predict(model)
    n, k = size(Xm)
    H    = Xm' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ) * Xm
    meat = Xm' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal((yv .- μ).^2) * Xm
    Hinv = LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(H)))
    V    = Hinv * meat * Hinv * (n / (n - k))
    return sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V))
end
df10c = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:, [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
se_rob = robust_se_glm(pois10, df10c, GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income), :docvis)
β = GLM.coef(pois10); cn = GLM.coefnames(pois10)
println("\n— Poisson with robust SE —")
for i in eachindex(β)
    @printf("%-12s coef=%10.6f  rob.se=%10.6f  z=%7.2f\n",
            cn[i], β[i], se_rob[i], β[i]/se_rob[i])
end
=#
```

### 10.3.7 The glm command

```{julia}
# glm docvis private chronic female income, family(poisson) link(log) vce(robust)
m_glm10 = glm_stata(df10,
    @formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    family = :poisson,
    link   = :log,
    vce    = :robust);
```

```{julia}
# gmm (docvis - exp({xb:private chronic female income}+{b0})),   ///
#   instruments(firmsize chronic female income) onestep nolog
#
# Nonlinear IV: E[(docvis - exp(x'β + b0)) · z] = 0 with z = (firmsize,
# chronic, female, income, _cons). Just-identified (5 params = 5 instruments).
m_gmm10 = gmm_stata(df10, :docvis,
    [:private, :chronic, :female, :income],     # regressors in linear index
    [:firmsize, :chronic, :female, :income];    # instruments
    mean_fn = :exp,
    onestep = true);
```

### 10.3.8 Other estimators

## 10.4 Different estimates of the VCE

### 10.4.1 General framework

### 10.4.2 Standard error options

### 10.4.3 Application

```{julia}
# Different VCE estimates after Poisson regression
# (Stata: poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(...))
#
# This whole chapter (§10.4 — §10.6) shares one fitted Poisson. We get it
# from `poisson_stata` (which already returns β / V / X in Stata-display
# order — slopes first, _cons last — and applies the n/(n-1) robust
# correction). Re-running this chunk after editing chunk 1 reloads
# everything downstream chunks need.

s10            = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)   # also prints reg table
df10c_v, β_v   = s10.df, s10.β
V_rob, X_v     = s10.V,  s10.X
n_v,    k_v    = s10.n,  s10.k
y_v            = Float64.(df10c_v.docvis)
μ_v             = exp.(X_v * β_v)
u_v             = y_v .- μ_v

# OIM: V = (X' diag(μ) X)⁻¹
V_oim = LinearAlgebra.inv(X_v' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_v) * X_v)
# OPG: V = (X' diag(u²) X)⁻¹  (outer-product of gradients)
M_opg = X_v' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(u_v.^2) * X_v
V_opg = LinearAlgebra.inv(M_opg)
# Cluster on age (Stata small-sample correction (G/(G-1)) · ((n-1)/(n-k)))
ages_v = Float64.(df10c_v.age)
S_clu  = zeros(k_v, k_v)
for g in unique(ages_v)
    idx = findall(ages_v .== g)
    s_g = X_v[idx, :]' * u_v[idx]
    S_clu .+= s_g * s_g'
end
G_v      = length(unique(ages_v))
dof_corr = (G_v / (G_v - 1)) * ((n_v - 1) / (n_v - k_v))
V_clu    = V_oim * S_clu * V_oim * dof_corr
# Bootstrap, reps = 400 (Julia's RNG differs from Stata, so this column will
# not match Stata's vce(boot) numbers exactly even with the same seed).
Random.seed!(10101)
B_v = 400
βb  = zeros(B_v, k_v)
for b in 1:B_v
    idx  = StatsBase.sample(1:n_v, n_v; replace = true)
    sub  = df10c_v[idx, :]
    m_b  = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
                   sub, Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
    βb[b, :] = vcat(GLM.coef(m_b)[2:end], GLM.coef(m_b)[1])
end
V_boot = StatsBase.cov(βb)

ses = (
    VCE_oim  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_oim)),
    VCE_opg  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_opg)),
    VCE_rob  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_rob)),
    VCE_clu  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_clu)),
    VCE_boot = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_boot)),
)

varnames_v = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "_cons"]
colhead_v  = ["VCE_oim", "VCE_opg", "VCE_rob", "VCE_clu", "VCE_boot"]

function _print_vce_row(label, vals)
    Printf.@printf("%12s |", label)
    for (j, v) in enumerate(vals)
        j == 1 ? Printf.@printf(" %8.4f", v) : Printf.@printf("%11.4f", v)
    end
    println("  ")
end

println("-"^69)
println("    Variable | VCE_oim    VCE_opg    VCE_rob    VCE_clu    VCE_boot  ")
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^55)
for (i, v) in enumerate(varnames_v)
    _print_vce_row(v, fill(β_v[i], length(colhead_v)))
    _print_vce_row("", [ses[Symbol(c)][i] for c in colhead_v])
end
println("-"^69)
```

## 10.5 Prediction

### 10.5.1 The predict equivalents

### 10.5.2 Application

```{julia}
# Predicted mean number of doctor visits using predict and predictnl
# (Stata: poisson ..., vce(robust); predict muhat if e(sample), n;
#  predictnl muhat2 = exp(_b[private]·private + ... + _b[_cons]),
#  se(semuhat2); summarize docvis muhat muhat2 semuhat2)

s = setup_mus10(df10)        # self-contained: refits via the chunk-1 helper
muhat    = exp.(s.X * s.β)               # = predict, n
muhat2   = copy(muhat)                    # predictnl: exp(linear index)
# Delta-method SE: ∂μ/∂β = μ·x, so SE(μ̂) = μ · sqrt(x' V x)
semuhat2 = [μ * sqrt(x' * s.V * x) for (μ, x) in zip(muhat2, eachrow(s.X))]

summarize_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(
    docvis   = s.df.docvis,
    muhat    = muhat,
    muhat2   = muhat2,
    semuhat2 = semuhat2))
```

### 10.5.3 Out-of-sample prediction

```{julia}
# (Stata: predict resid, score; gen resid2 = docvis - muhat; summarize ...)
#
# For Poisson with log link, the score residual ∂lnL_i/∂(x_iβ) is exactly
# y_i − μ_i, identical to docvis − muhat. In Stata the two means appear
# different (-5.25e-08 vs -4.86e-08) only because Stata stores `muhat` at
# slightly lower precision before the subtraction; in Julia both are the
# same array. Either way both means are mathematically zero — Σ(y − μ) = 0
# is the Poisson MLE first-order condition for the constant.

s = setup_mus10(df10)
muhat  = exp.(s.X * s.β)
y_     = Float64.(s.df.docvis)
resid  = y_ .- muhat
resid2 = y_ .- muhat

summarize_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(resid = resid, resid2 = resid2))
```

### 10.5.4 Out-of-sample prediction with year01 data

```{julia}
# Out-of-sample prediction for year01 data using year02 estimates
# (Stata: poisson ... if year02==1, vce(robust); keep if year01==1;
#  predict muhatyear01, n; summarize docvis muhatyear01)

s = setup_mus10(df10)        # year02 fit (df10 is already filtered to year02)

# Reload mus10data and subset to year01 to apply year02 coefficients OOS
df10_full = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("musr/mus10data.dta"))
for c in names(df10_full)
    if eltype(df10_full[!, c]) <: Union{Missing, Float32} || eltype(df10_full[!, c]) === Float32
        df10_full[!, c] = convert.(Union{Missing, Float64}, df10_full[!, c])
    end
end
df01  = filter(r -> r.year01 == 1, df10_full)
df01c = DataFrames.dropmissing(df01[:, [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
X01   = hcat(Float64.(df01c.private),
             Float64.(df01c.chronic),
             Float64.(df01c.female),
             Float64.(df01c.income),
             ones(DataFrames.nrow(df01c)))
muhatyear01 = exp.(X01 * s.β)

summarize_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(
    docvis      = df01c.docvis,
    muhatyear01 = muhatyear01))
```

### 10.5.5 Prediction at specified regressor values

```{julia}
# Counterfactual prediction: set private = 1 for everyone in year02 sample
# (Stata: keep if year02==1; quietly poisson ..., vce(robust);
#  preserve; replace private = 1; predict muhatpeq1, n;
#  summarize muhatpeq1; restore)

s = setup_mus10(df10)
X_pe1 = hcat(ones(s.n),                         # private = 1 for all
             Float64.(s.df.chronic),
             Float64.(s.df.female),
             Float64.(s.df.income),
             ones(s.n))                         # _cons
muhatpeq1 = exp.(X_pe1 * s.β)

summarize_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(muhatpeq1 = muhatpeq1))
```

```{julia}
# Prediction at a specified value of all the regressors via delta method
# (Stata: nlcom exp(_b[_cons] + _b[private]·1 + _b[chronic]·0
#                   + _b[female]·1 + _b[income]·10))
#
# β_v order is [private, chronic, female, income, _cons], so the regressor
# vector at the chosen point is (1, 0, 1, 10, 1).

s = setup_mus10(df10)
nlcom_stata(b -> exp(b[1]*1 + b[2]*0 + b[3]*1 + b[4]*10 + b[5]),
            s.β, s.V; depvar = "docvis")
```

```{julia}
# Linear-combination prediction at a specified value of all regressors,
# reported on the exp scale (Stata: lincom _cons + private·1 + chronic·0
#                                    + female·1 + income·10, eform)
#
# Compared with `nlcom exp(...)`: lincom builds the SE on the *linear*
# combination L = w'β and reports z = L/SE(L); with `eform` the point
# estimate is exp(L), the SE is exp(L)·SE(L) (delta method, same as nlcom),
# but the CI is the *asymmetric* exp(L ± z·SE(L)). That's why z here is 17.89
# and the CI is (2.656, 3.378), vs nlcom's z=16.31 and CI (2.635, 3.355) on
# the same expression.

s = setup_mus10(df10)
lincom_stata([1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 10.0, 1.0], s.β, s.V;
             eform = true, depvar = "docvis")
```

### 10.5.6 Prediction of other quantities

```{julia}
# (Stata: poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust); margins)
# The Poisson regression table is printed by setup_mus10(...; quiet=false)
# and the overall predictive margin follows.
s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)
margins_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X)
```

```{julia}
# Sample average prediction with private = 1 for everyone
# (Stata: margins, at(private=1))
s = setup_mus10(df10)
X_pe1 = hcat(ones(s.n), Float64.(s.df.chronic), Float64.(s.df.female),
             Float64.(s.df.income), ones(s.n))
margins_stata(s.β, s.V, X_pe1)
```

```{julia}
# Adjusted prediction at a specified point of all regressors
# (Stata: margins, at(private=1 chronic=0 female=1 income=10))
s = setup_mus10(df10)
margins_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
              at        = [1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 10.0, 1.0],
              at_labels = [:private => 1, :chronic => 0,
                           :female  => 1, :income  => 10])
```


```{julia}
# Sample average prediction at each level of indicator variable private
# (Stata: poisson docvis i.private chronic female income, vce(robust);
#  margins private)
s = setup_mus10(df10)
margins_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X; over = "private" => 1)
```

### 10.6.1 Calculus and finite-difference methods

### 10.6.5 Manual marginal-effects helpers

### 10.6.6 MEM: Marginal effect at mean

```{julia}
# Marginal effects at the mean (MEM)
# (Stata: poisson docvis i.private i.chronic i.female income, vce(robust);
#  margins, dydx(*) atmean)

s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)        # also prints regression table
margins_dydx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2), ("female", 3)],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

#### Comparison of calculus and finite-difference methods

```{julia}
# Calculus-method MEM (no `i.` factor prefixes — every regressor is treated
# as continuous, so dy/dx_j = β_j · μ(x̄) for every j, including the binary
# private/chronic/female).
# (Stata: poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust);
#  margins, dydx(*) atmean)

s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)
margins_dydx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2),
                  ("female",  3), ("income",  4)])
```

### 10.6.7 MER: Marginal effect at representative value

```{julia}
# MER — marginal effect at a representative point
# (Stata: poisson docvis i.private i.chronic i.female income, vce(robust);
#  margins, dydx(*) at(private=1 chronic=0 female=1 income=10) noatlegend)

s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)
margins_dydx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    factors    = [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2), ("female", 3)],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    at         = [1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 10.0, 1.0],
    noatlegend = true)
```

### 10.6.8 AME: Average marginal effect

```{julia}
# AME — average marginal effects across the sample
# (Stata: poisson docvis i.private i.chronic i.female income, vce(robust);
#  margins, dydx(*))

s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)
margins_dydx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    factors    = [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2), ("female", 3)],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

```{julia}
# Usual ME for a single continuous regressor evaluated at sample means
# (Stata: quietly poisson ..., vce(robust);
#  margins, dydx(income) atmean noatlegend)
#
# Same MEM math as §10.6.6 but (a) only `income` (continuous), (b) no
# factors, (c) noatlegend=true to suppress the At: block.

s = setup_mus10(df10)
margins_dydx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    noatlegend = true)
```

### 10.6.9 Elasticities and semielasticities

```{julia}
# Elasticity of E[y] w.r.t. income at the sample-mean point
# (Stata: margins, eyex(income) atmean noatlegend)
#
# For Poisson with log link: ε = β_income · x̄_income (independent of μ).
# Reuses margins_dydx_stata with kind=:eyex.

s = setup_mus10(df10)
margins_dydx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :eyex,
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    noatlegend = true)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Semielasticity evaluated at mean of regressors
margins, eydx(income) atmean noatlegend
=#
s = setup_mus10(df10)
margins_dydx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :eydx,
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    noatlegend = true)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Other semielasticity evaluated at mean of regressors
margins, dyex(income) atmean noatlegend
=#
s = setup_mus10(df10)
margins_dydx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :dyex,
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    noatlegend = true)
```

```{julia}
# Stata's legacy mfx output (older/textbook form of margins, dydx atmean)
# (Stata: mfx, dydx varlist(income))

s = setup_mus10(df10)
mfx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

```{julia}
# Stata's legacy mfx with elasticity option — same layout as `mfx, dydx`
# but the column reports ε(x) = β_j · x_j instead of dy/dx
# (Stata: mfx, eyex varlist(income))

s = setup_mus10(df10)
mfx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :eyex,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

```{julia}
# Stata's legacy mfx with semi-elasticity option: ∂lnE[y]/∂x = β_j (Poisson
# log link), so the reported value is just the coefficient itself.
# (Stata: mfx, eydx varlist(income))

s = setup_mus10(df10)
mfx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :eydx,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

```{julia}
# Stata's legacy mfx with dy/ex (unit change in y per percent change in x)
# (Stata: mfx, dyex varlist(income))
#
# For Poisson with log link: dy/ex = β_j · μ · x_j (depends on both μ and x)

s = setup_mus10(df10)
mfx_stata(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :dyex,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

### 10.6.10 AME computed manually

```{julia}
# AME for income computed manually via finite differences:
#   memanual_i = (μ̂(income_i + 0.01) − μ̂(income_i)) / 0.01
# Should be ≈ β_income · μ̂_i for small h, so its mean ≈ AME(income).
# (Stata: predict mu0,n; replace income=income+0.01; predict mu1,n;
#  gen memanual = (mu1-mu0)/0.01; summarize memanual)

s = setup_mus10(df10)
μ0 = exp.(s.X * s.β)                          # predict, n  (income at original)
X1 = copy(s.X); X1[:, 4] .+= 0.01             # bump income by 0.01
μ1 = exp.(X1 * s.β)                           # predict, n  (after bump)
memanual = (μ1 .- μ0) ./ 0.01

summarize_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(memanual = memanual))
```

```{julia}
# AME computed manually for ALL regressors using a per-variable
# finite-difference step delta_v = sd(v)/1000.
# (Stata: foreach var of varlist $xlist { ... gen me_`var' = (mu1-mu0)/delta ... }
#         summarize me_*)
#
# These are the *calculus*-method per-obs MEs (≈ β_v · μ_i for log link),
# not the discrete-change ones — for binary regressors they differ from
# `margins, dydx(*)` AME values (see §10.6.8).

s = setup_mus10(df10)
μ0 = exp.(s.X * s.β)
df_me = DataFrames.DataFrame()
for (nm, idx) in [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2),
                  ("female",  3), ("income",  4)]
    delta = Statistics.std(s.X[:, idx]) / 1000
    X1    = copy(s.X); X1[:, idx] .+= delta
    μ1    = exp.(X1 * s.β)
    df_me[!, "me_$nm"] = (μ1 .- μ0) ./ delta
end

summarize_stata(df_me)
```

### 10.6.11 Polynomial regressors

```{julia}
# AME for a polynomial regressor via the chain rule
# (Stata: gen inc2 = income^2; gen inc3 = income^3;
#  poisson docvis private chronic female income inc2 inc3, vce(robust);
#  predict muhat,n; gen me_income = muhat*(β_inc + 2·β_inc2·income + 3·β_inc3·inc2);
#  summarize me_income)
#
# For Poisson with log link: ∂μ/∂income = μ · ∂lnμ/∂income
#   = μ · (β_income + 2·β_inc2·income + 3·β_inc3·income²)

dfp = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
dfp.inc2 = dfp.income .^ 2
dfp.inc3 = dfp.income .^ 3

fit_poly = poisson_stata(dfp,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + inc2 + inc3);
    vce = :robust);

# β order in Stata-display layout: [private, chronic, female, income, inc2, inc3, _cons]
β_v   = fit_poly.β
X_v   = fit_poly.X
μ_hat = exp.(X_v * β_v)
inc   = X_v[:, 4]

me_income = μ_hat .* (β_v[4] .+ 2 .* β_v[5] .* inc .+ 3 .* β_v[6] .* inc.^2)

summarize_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(me_income = me_income))
```

### 10.6.12 Interacted regressors

### 10.6.13 Complex interactions and nonlinearities

```{julia}
# AME of `income` from the cubic-in-income Poisson model, with delta-method SE
# (Stata: poisson docvis private chronic female c.income c.income#c.income
#                  c.income#c.income#c.income, vce(robust);
#  margins, dydx(income))
#
# Same point estimate as §10.6.11 (mean of the per-obs ME) but the SE here
# accounts for the fact that `income` enters the linear index in three
# different terms — the gradient combines the chain-rule contribution from
# μ (which depends on all β) and the polynomial-coefficient slots.

dfp = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
dfp.inc2 = dfp.income .^ 2
dfp.inc3 = dfp.income .^ 3

fit_poly = poisson_stata(dfp,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + inc2 + inc3);
    vce = :robust, quiet = true)
β = fit_poly.β; V = fit_poly.V; X = fit_poly.X; n_p = fit_poly.n; k_p = fit_poly.k
inc       = X[:, 4]
μ         = exp.(X * β)
poly_term = β[4] .+ 2 .* β[5] .* inc .+ 3 .* β[6] .* inc.^2
me_i      = μ .* poly_term
ame       = Statistics.mean(me_i)

# Delta-method gradient: ∂AME/∂β = mean over i of [μ_i · poly_term_i · x_i + μ_i · poly_grad_i]
# where poly_grad_i has 1 at the income slot, 2·inc_i at inc2, 3·inc_i² at inc3.
g     = vec((μ .* poly_term)' * X) ./ n_p
g[4] += sum(μ)               / n_p
g[5] += sum(μ .* 2 .* inc)   / n_p
g[6] += sum(μ .* 3 .* inc.^2) / n_p
se    = sqrt(g' * V * g)
z     = ame / se
p     = 2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), abs(z)))
crit  = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Normal(), 0.975)
lo, hi = ame - crit*se, ame + crit*se

g9 = function(x; w_::Int=10, sig::Int=7)
    sig_use = sig
    s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 10 : 9
    while length(s) > cap && sig_use > 1
        sig_use -= 1
        s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    end
    0 < abs(x) < 1 && (s = replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1."))
    lpad(s, w_)
end

println()
Printf.@printf("Average marginal effects                                 Number of obs = %s\n",
               replace(string(n_p), r"(\d)(?=(\d{3})+$)" => s"\1,"))
println("Model VCE: Robust\n")
println("Expression: Predicted number of events, predict()")
println("dy/dx wrt:  income\n")
println("-"^78)
println("             |            Delta-method")
println("             |      dy/dx   std. err.      z    P>|z|     [95% conf. interval]")
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^64)
Printf.@printf("%12s | %s  %s  %s   %s    %s  %s\n",
               "income", g9(ame; w_=10), g9(se; w_=9),
               Printf.@sprintf("%7.2f", z),
               Printf.@sprintf("%.3f", p),
               g9(lo; w_=9), g9(hi; w_=10))
println("-"^78)
```

```{julia}
# Poisson with interaction female × income — uses `poisson_stata`'s
# `layout` keyword to reproduce Stata's multi-line interaction header
# (`female# / c.income / 1`).
# (Stata: poisson docvis private chronic i.female c.income i.female#c.income,
#         vce(robust) nolog)

df_intr = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])

poisson_stata(df_intr,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + female & income);
    vce = :robust,
    layout = [
        "private"  => "private",
        "chronic"  => "chronic",
        "1.female" => "female",
        "income"   => "income",
        :blank,
        ["      female#", "    c.income ", "1"] => "female & income",
        :blank,
        "_cons"    => "(Intercept)",
    ]);
```

```{julia}
# AME for the interaction model — `margins_dydx_stata` now takes an
# `interactions` keyword that tells it which X column is the product of
# which base columns. With this, switching female 0↔1 also rebuilds the
# `female × income` column, and ∂μ/∂income picks up the β_intr · female
# contribution. Without `interactions`, the helper would silently treat
# the interaction column as independent of female and income, producing
# the wrong AME.
# (Stata: margins, dydx(female income))

df_intr = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
fit_intr = poisson_stata(df_intr,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + female & income);
    vce = :robust, quiet = true)

margins_dydx_stata(fit_intr.β, fit_intr.V, fit_intr.X;
    factors      = [("female", 3)],
    continuous   = [("income", 4)],
    interactions = [(col = 5, base = [3, 4])])  # col 5 = female × income
```

```{julia}
# AME for income computed manually for the interaction model:
# bumping `income` also has to rebuild the `female × income` column
# (Stata: preserve; predict mu0,n; replace income = income + delta;
#  replace inc2 = income^2; replace inc3 = income^3; predict mu1,n;
#  gen me_inc = (mu1 - mu0)/delta; summarize me_inc; restore)
#
# The `replace inc2`/`inc3` lines in the Stata code are leftovers from a
# prior cubic-in-income setup — they don't affect the result here because
# the FITTED model is `private + chronic + i.female + c.income +
# i.female#c.income`, which has no inc2/inc3 terms. What matters in Julia
# is rebuilding the `female × income` column after bumping income.

df_intr = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
fit_intr = poisson_stata(df_intr,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + female & income);
    vce = :robust, quiet = true)
β_v = fit_intr.β; X_v = fit_intr.X    # cols: 1=priv 2=chr 3=fem 4=inc 5=fem×inc 6=_cons

μ0    = exp.(X_v * β_v)
delta = Statistics.std(X_v[:, 4]) / 100
X1    = copy(X_v)
X1[:, 4] .+= delta                    # bump income
X1[:, 5]  .= X1[:, 3] .* X1[:, 4]     # rebuild female × income
μ1    = exp.(X1 * β_v)
me_inc = (μ1 .- μ0) ./ delta

summarize_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(me_inc = me_inc))
```

## 10.7 Model diagnostics

### 10.7.1 Goodness-of-fit measures

```{julia}
# Pseudo-R² = 1 − ℓ(β̂) / ℓ_0  (McFadden's, the same `e(ll)/e(ll_0)` Stata
# uses). `poisson_stata` already returns it as `.pseudo_r2`.
# (Stata: quietly poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust);
#  display "Pseudo-R^2 = " 1 - e(ll)/e(ll_0))

s = setup_mus10(df10)
Printf.@printf("Pseudo-R^2 = %.7f\n", s.fit.pseudo_r2)
```

### 10.7.2 Information criteria for model comparison

```{julia}
# AIC / BIC after Poisson regression  (Stata: estat ic)
s = setup_mus10(df10)
estat_ic_stata(s.fit);
```

### 10.7.3 Residuals

```{julia}
# Four residual flavors after Poisson GLM
# (Stata: glm docvis private chronic female income, family(poisson);
#  predict mu, mu; gen uraw = docvis - mu; predict upearson, pearson;
#  predict udeviance, deviance; predict uanscombe, anscombe;
#  summarize uraw upearson udeviance uanscombe)
#
# Formulas for Poisson:
#   raw      : u_i = y_i − μ̂_i
#   Pearson  : (y_i − μ̂_i) / √μ̂_i
#   Deviance : sign(y_i − μ̂_i) · √(2{y_i·log(y_i/μ̂_i) − (y_i − μ̂_i)})
#              with y_i·log(y_i/μ̂_i) ≡ 0 when y_i = 0
#   Anscombe : (3/2) · (y_i^(2/3) − μ̂_i^(2/3)) / μ̂_i^(1/6)

s = setup_mus10(df10)
y = Float64.(s.df.docvis)
μ = exp.(s.X * s.β)

uraw     = y .- μ
upearson = (y .- μ) ./ sqrt.(μ)
udev     = [sign(y[i] - μ[i]) *
            sqrt(2 * (y[i] > 0 ? y[i]*log(y[i]/μ[i]) - (y[i] - μ[i])
                                : -(y[i] - μ[i])))
            for i in eachindex(y)]
uans     = (3/2) .* (y.^(2/3) .- μ.^(2/3)) ./ μ.^(1/6)

summarize_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(
    uraw      = uraw,
    upearson  = upearson,
    udeviance = udev,
    uanscombe = uans))
```

### 10.7.4 Model-specification tests

```{julia}
# Pairwise correlations of the four residual flavors from the previous chunk
# (Stata: correlate uraw upearson udeviance uanscombe — not in the book)
correlate_stata(DataFrames.DataFrame(
    uraw      = uraw,
    upearson  = upearson,
    udeviance = udev,
    uanscombe = uans))
```

# 11 Nonlinear optimization methods

## 11.1 Introduction

## 11.2 Newton-Raphson method

### 11.2.1 NR method

### 11.2.2 NR method for Poisson

### 11.2.3 Poisson NR example using native Julia matrix algebra

#### Core Julia code for Poisson NR iterations

```{julia}
# Pseudocode for Newton-Raphson Poisson MLE
# (X is n×k, y is n×1, b is k×1; tolerance = 1e-16)
#
#   b   = zeros(k);  iter = 0;  cha = 1
#   while cha > 1e-16
#       μ    = exp.(X * b)
#       grad = X' * (y .- μ)            # k×1 score
#       hes  = X' * Diagonal(μ) * X     # k×k negative Hessian
#       bold = b
#       b    = bold + cholesky(Symmetric(hes)) \ grad
#       cha  = sum((bold .- b).^2) / sum(bold.^2)
#       iter += 1
#   end
```

#### Complete Julia code for Poisson NR iterations

```{julia}
# Set up data and design matrix
df11 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("musr/mus10data.dta"))
df11 = filter(r -> r.year02 == 1, df11)
y11 = Float64.(df11.docvis)
X11 = hcat(Float64.(df11.private), Float64.(df11.chronic),
           Float64.(df11.female),  Float64.(df11.income),
           ones(DataFrames.nrow(df11)))
xnms11 = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "_cons"]
n11, k11 = size(X11)
@printf("n = %d, k = %d\n", n11, k11)
```

```{julia}
# Newton-Raphson loop
function poisson_nr(X, y; tol = 1e-16)
    n, k = size(X)
    b = zeros(k); iter = 0; cha = 1.0
    while cha > tol && iter < 100
        μ    = exp.(X * b)
        grad = X' * (y .- μ)
        hes  = X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ) * X
        bold = copy(b)
        b    = bold .+ LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(hes)) \ grad
        cha  = sum((bold .- b).^2) / sum(bold.^2 .+ eps())
        iter += 1
        @printf("iter = %2d  cha = %12.5g\n", iter, cha)
    end
    # Robust sandwich SE
    μ     = exp.(X * b)
    hes   = X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ) * X
    vgrad = X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal((y .- μ).^2) * X
    Hinv  = try
        LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(hes)))
    catch
        LinearAlgebra.pinv(hes)
    end
    V     = Hinv * vgrad * Hinv * (n / (n - k))
    return b, V, iter
end

b11, V11, iter11 = poisson_nr(X11, y11)
```

```{julia}
# Present results in a coefficient table
se11  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V11))
z11   = b11 ./ se11
p11   = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z11)))
println("─" ^ 70)
@printf("%-10s %12s %12s %8s %8s\n", "", "Coef.", "Rob.SE", "z", "P>|z|")
println("─" ^ 70)
for i in 1:k11
    @printf("%-10s %12.6f %12.6f %8.2f %8.4f\n",
            xnms11[i], b11[i], se11[i], z11[i], p11[i])
end
println("─" ^ 70)
@printf("Iterations = %d, n = %d\n", iter11, n11)
```

```{julia}
# Cross-check with GLM.jl
GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
        df11, Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
```

```{julia}
# Verbose Poisson MLE via raw Newton–Raphson, showing parameter vector and
# gradient at every iteration — for comparison with the standard fit above.
# (Stata: poisson docvis private chronic female income, log trace gradient
#         iterate(100) tol(1e-4) ltol(0) robust)
#
# Score   ∇ℓ(β) = X'(y − μ),    Hessian H(β) = −X' diag(μ) X.
# Update  β_{k+1} = β_k + (X' diag(μ) X)⁻¹ · X'(y − μ).
# Stops when max |Δβ_j| / (|β_j| + ε) < 1e-4 (Stata's tol(...)).

dfc = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
y_v = Float64.(dfc.docvis)
X_v = hcat(Float64.(dfc.private), Float64.(dfc.chronic),
           Float64.(dfc.female),  Float64.(dfc.income),
           ones(DataFrames.nrow(dfc)))
varnames_v = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "_cons"]
n_v, k_v   = size(X_v)
depvar_v   = "docvis"
ll_const   = sum(SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y_v .+ 1))

# Stata-style %9.0g for one number (sign-aware cap so negative |x|<1 keeps
# 7 sig digs after the leading-zero strip).
_g = function (x; sig::Int=7)
    sig_use = sig
    s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 10 : 9
    while length(s) > cap && sig_use > 1
        sig_use -= 1
        s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    end
    0 < abs(x) < 1 && (s = replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1."))
    s
end

function _print_iter(iter, β, ll, g)
    println("-"^81)
    Printf.@printf("Iteration %d:\n", iter)
    println("Parameter vector:")
    print("  ")
    for _ in 1:k_v
        Printf.@printf("%11s", "$(depvar_v):")
    end
    println()
    print("  ")
    for nm in varnames_v
        Printf.@printf("%11s", nm)
    end
    println()
    print("r1")
    for j in 1:k_v
        Printf.@printf("%11s", _g(β[j]))
    end
    println()
    println()
    Printf.@printf("%48sLog pseudolikelihood = %s\n", "", _g(ll))
    Printf.@printf("Gradient vector (length = %s):\n", _g(sqrt(sum(g.^2))))
    print("  ")
    for _ in 1:k_v
        Printf.@printf("%11s", "$(depvar_v):")
    end
    println()
    print("  ")
    for nm in varnames_v
        Printf.@printf("%11s", nm)
    end
    println()
    print("r1")
    for j in 1:k_v
        Printf.@printf("%11s", _g(g[j]))
    end
    println()
end

# Newton–Raphson loop
β_nr = zeros(k_v)
for iter in 0:100
    μ_nr = exp.(X_v * β_nr)
    ll_nr = sum(y_v .* log.(μ_nr) .- μ_nr) - ll_const
    g_nr  = X_v' * (y_v .- μ_nr)
    _print_iter(iter, β_nr, ll_nr, g_nr)

    H_nr = X_v' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_nr) * X_v
    Δ    = H_nr \ g_nr
    if maximum(abs.(Δ) ./ (abs.(β_nr) .+ 1e-10)) < 1e-4
        β_nr .+= Δ
        break
    end
    β_nr .+= Δ
end
println("-"^81)

# Print the final regression table in Stata's standard layout
poisson_stata(dfc,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :robust);
```

```{julia}
# Objective function with multiple optima:
#   Q(θ) = 100 − 1e-7 · (θ−10)(θ−30)(θ−50)²(θ−70)(θ−80),  θ ∈ [5, 90]
# Three local optima visible in the range — illustrates why gradient
# methods need good starting values.
# (Stata: graph twoway function y = 100 - 1e-7 * ..., range(5 90) ...)

θ = collect(range(5.0, 90.0; length = 401))
Q = @. 100 - 1e-7 * (θ - 10) * (θ - 30) * (θ - 50)^2 * (θ - 70) * (θ - 80)

fig = CairoMakie.Figure()
ax  = CairoMakie.Axis(fig[1, 1];
        xlabel = "Theta", ylabel = "Q(theta)",
        title  = "Objective function Q(theta) as theta varies")
CairoMakie.lines!(ax, θ, Q; color = :steelblue, linewidth = 2)
CairoMakie.save("musr/mus11fig1.png", fig)
fig
```
