### 11.3.6 Numerical derivatives

## 11.4 The Optim.jl approach: lf-style log-likelihood

### 11.4.1 Optim.jl interface

### 11.4.2 lf-style evaluators

### 11.4.3 Poisson example: Single-index model

```{julia}
# Poisson negative log-likelihood (excluding constant lnΓ(y+1))
function poisson_nll(β, X, y)
    η = X * β
    μ = exp.(η)
    -sum(.-μ .+ y .* η .- SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y .+ 1))
end
```

```{julia}
# Optim with NewtonTrustRegion for Poisson MLE — robust SEs separately
β0 = zeros(k11)
res_pois = Optim.optimize(β -> poisson_nll(β, X11, y11), β0,
                          Optim.LBFGS(); autodiff = :forward)
println(res_pois)
β_pois = Optim.minimizer(res_pois)
println("Coefficients: ", round.(β_pois, digits = 6))
```

```{julia}
# Compute robust SE manually (sandwich)
μ_p = exp.(X11 * β_pois)
H   = X11' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_p) * X11
S   = X11' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal((y11 .- μ_p).^2) * X11
V_p = try
        LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(H))) * S *
              LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(H)))
    catch
        LinearAlgebra.pinv(H) * S * LinearAlgebra.pinv(H)
    end *
      (n11 / (n11 - k11))
se_p = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_p))
println("\n— Optim.jl Poisson MLE with robust SE —")
for i in 1:k11
    @printf("%-10s %12.6f  se=%10.6f\n", xnms11[i], β_pois[i], se_p[i])
end
```

```{julia}
# Compute log-likelihood at optimum
@printf("Log-likelihood = %.4f\n", -Optim.minimum(res_pois))
```

```{julia}
# Poisson MLE via a user-supplied log-likelihood — the Julia analogue of
# Stata's `ml model lf lfpois ...; ml check; ml search; ml maximize`.
# The "lfpois evaluator" is the closure `lfpois(β)` below; it returns the
# total log-likelihood (Optim minimizes the negative).
# (Stata: program lfpois; args lnf theta1; ...;
#         ml model lf lfpois (docvis = private chronic female income), vce(robust);
#         ml check; ml search; ml maximize)

dfc = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
y_v = Float64.(dfc.docvis)
X_v = hcat(Float64.(dfc.private), Float64.(dfc.chronic),
           Float64.(dfc.female),  Float64.(dfc.income),
           ones(DataFrames.nrow(dfc)))
n_v, k_v = size(X_v)
ll_const = sum(SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y_v .+ 1))

# Stata's `lfpois` evaluator written as a single Julia function:
#     ln f_i(β) = -μ_i + y_i · θ_i - ln(y_i!)   where  θ_i = x_i'β,  μ_i = exp(θ_i)
# Returned as the SUM (total log-likelihood); Optim feeds the negative.
function lfpois(β)
    θ = X_v * β
    μ = exp.(θ)
    return sum(y_v .* θ .- μ) - ll_const
end

# Newton-Raphson, mirroring `ml maximize`'s iteration log
println("Initial:      Log pseudolikelihood = ",
        Printf.@sprintf("%.3f", lfpois(zeros(k_v))))
β_ml = zeros(k_v)
for iter in 0:100
    Printf.@printf("Iteration %d:  Log pseudolikelihood = %.3f\n",
                   iter, lfpois(β_ml))
    μ_it = exp.(X_v * β_ml)
    g_it = X_v' * (y_v .- μ_it)
    H_it = X_v' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_it) * X_v
    Δ    = H_it \ g_it
    if maximum(abs.(Δ) ./ (abs.(β_ml) .+ 1e-10)) < 1e-4
        β_ml .+= Δ
        break
    end
    β_ml .+= Δ
end
println()

# `ml maximize, vce(robust)` produces the regression table — same format
# as `poisson_stata`, since the converged β is identical.
poisson_stata(dfc,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :robust);
```

### 11.4.4 Negative binomial example: Two-index model

```{julia}
# Negative binomial MLE: parameters are (β, α). Use log(α) for unconstrained.
function negbin_nll(params, X, y)
    p = size(X, 2)
    β = params[1:p]
    α = exp(params[p + 1])
    η = X * β
    μ = exp.(η)
    inv_α = 1 / α
    ll = sum(SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y .+ inv_α) .-
             SpecialFunctions.loggamma(inv_α) .-
             SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y .+ 1) .-
             (y .+ inv_α) .* log.(1 .+ α .* μ) .+
             y .* log(α) .+ y .* η)
    return -ll
end

res_nb = Optim.optimize(params -> negbin_nll(params, X11, y11),
                        vcat(β_pois, log(1.0)),
                        Optim.LBFGS(); autodiff = :forward)
β_nb = Optim.minimizer(res_nb)
α_nb = exp(β_nb[end])
println("\n— NegBin MLE coefficients —")
for i in 1:k11
    @printf("%-10s %12.6f\n", xnms11[i], β_nb[i])
end
@printf("alpha     = %.6f\n", α_nb)
@printf("logL      = %.4f\n", -Optim.minimum(res_nb))
```

```{julia}
# Negative-binomial MLE via a user-supplied two-equation log-likelihood,
# matching Stata's `ml model lf lfnb (docvis = private chronic female income) ()`
# eq1 = β (linear index for μ);  eq2 = α (NB2 dispersion, fitted DIRECTLY,
# not log α — same as Stata's lfnb).
# (Stata: program lfnb; args lnf theta1 a; ...;
#         ml model lf lfnb (docvis = ...) (); ml maximize, nolog)

dfc = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
y_v = Float64.(dfc.docvis)
X_v = hcat(Float64.(dfc.private), Float64.(dfc.chronic),
           Float64.(dfc.female),  Float64.(dfc.income),
           ones(DataFrames.nrow(dfc)))
n_v, kβ_v = size(X_v)
varnames_v = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "_cons"]

# Stata's lfnb evaluator written as a single Julia function (NB2, α direct):
#   ln f_i = lnΓ(y+1/α) − lnΓ(1/α) − lnΓ(y+1)
#            − (y+1/α)·ln(1+α·μ) + y·ln(α) + y·ln(μ),    μ = exp(x'β)
function lfnb_total(p)
    β = p[1:kβ_v]; α = p[end]
    α <= 0 && return -1e10
    μ = exp.(X_v * β)
    invα = 1 / α
    return sum(SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y_v .+ invα)
              .- SpecialFunctions.loggamma(invα)
              .- SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y_v .+ 1)
              .- (y_v .+ invα) .* log.(1 .+ α .* μ)
              .+ y_v .* log(α)
              .+ y_v .* log.(μ))
end

# Maximize via L-BFGS with autodiff (`ml maximize`'s default is BHHH-like;
# either converges to the same MLE).
const FD = Optim.NLSolversBase.ForwardDiff   # Optim already vendors ForwardDiff
p0   = vcat(zeros(kβ_v), 1.0)                 # β = 0, α = 1
res  = Optim.optimize(p -> -lfnb_total(p), p0, Optim.LBFGS();
                      autodiff = :forward)
p_ml = Optim.minimizer(res)
β_ml = p_ml[1:kβ_v]; α_ml = p_ml[end]
ll_max = -Optim.minimum(res)

# OIM standard errors from inverse Hessian of the negative log-likelihood
H_ml = FD.hessian(p -> -lfnb_total(p), p_ml)
V_ml = LinearAlgebra.inv(H_ml)
se_ml = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_ml))
z_ml  = p_ml ./ se_ml
pv_ml = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z_ml)))
crit  = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Normal(), 0.975)
lo_ml = p_ml .- crit .* se_ml
hi_ml = p_ml .+ crit .* se_ml

# Wald chi² on β slopes (excluding α and the eq1 intercept)
slope_idx = 1:4
W = β_ml[slope_idx]' * LinearAlgebra.inv(V_ml[slope_idx, slope_idx]) * β_ml[slope_idx]
W_p = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(length(slope_idx)), W)

# Stata-style table (two equations, eq1 / eq2 sub-headers)
g9 = function (x; w_::Int=10, sig::Int=7)
    sig_use = sig
    s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 10 : 9
    while length(s) > cap && sig_use > 1
        sig_use -= 1
        s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    end
    0 < abs(x) < 1 && (s = replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1."))
    lpad(s, w_)
end
commafmt(num::Integer) = begin
    s = string(abs(num)); parts = String[]; i = length(s)
    while i >= 1; push!(parts, s[max(1, i-2):i]); i -= 3; end
    (num < 0 ? "-" : "") * join(reverse(parts), ",")
end
function row(label, idx)
    Printf.@printf("%12s | %s  %s  %s   %s    %s  %s\n",
                   label, g9(p_ml[idx]; w_=10), g9(se_ml[idx]; w_=9),
                   Printf.@sprintf("%7.2f", z_ml[idx]),
                   Printf.@sprintf("%.3f", pv_ml[idx]),
                   g9(lo_ml[idx]; w_=9), g9(hi_ml[idx]; w_=10))
end

println()
Printf.@printf("%-56s%-13s = %6s\n", "", "Number of obs", commafmt(n_v))
Printf.@printf("%56s%-13s = %6.2f\n", "", "Wald chi2(4)", W)
Printf.@printf("Log likelihood = %.4f%23s%-13s = %6.4f\n",
               ll_max, "", "Prob > chi2", W_p)
println()
println("-"^78)
Printf.@printf("%12s | Coefficient  Std. err.      z    P>|z|     [95%% conf. interval]\n",
               "docvis")
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^64)
Printf.@printf("%-12s |\n", "eq1")
for i in 1:5
    row(varnames_v[i], i)
end
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^64)
Printf.@printf("%-12s |\n", "eq2")
row("_cons", 6)
println("-"^78)
```

### 11.4.5 NLS example: Nonlikelihood model

```{julia}
# NLS via the lf "pseudo-likelihood" trick — Stata's `ml model lf lfnls`
# treats lnf = -(y − exp(x'β))² as a per-observation log-likelihood, so
# maximizing Σ lnf is the same as minimizing the SSR of the Poisson mean
# function. Robust SE is the BHHH-style sandwich on the SSR's score.
# (Stata: program lfnls; args lnf theta1; replace lnf = -(y - exp(theta1))^2;
#         ml model lf lfnls (docvis = ...), vce(robust); ml maximize)

dfc = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
y_v = Float64.(dfc.docvis)
X_v = hcat(Float64.(dfc.private), Float64.(dfc.chronic),
           Float64.(dfc.female),  Float64.(dfc.income),
           ones(DataFrames.nrow(dfc)))
n_v, k_v = size(X_v)
varnames_v = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "_cons"]

# Stata's lfnls evaluator: total "pseudo-log-lik" is just −SSR
function lfnls_total(β)
    μ = exp.(X_v * β)
    return -sum((y_v .- μ).^2)
end

# Maximize via L-BFGS (mirrors Stata's `ml maximize`)
res    = Optim.optimize(β -> -lfnls_total(β), zeros(k_v), Optim.LBFGS(),
                        Optim.Options(g_tol = 1e-10, iterations = 500);
                        autodiff = :forward)
β_nls  = Optim.minimizer(res)
ll_max = lfnls_total(β_nls)              # = −SSR (Stata's "Log pseudolikelihood")

# Robust sandwich. Score s_i(β) = ∂lnf_i/∂β = 2 u_i μ_i x_i,  u_i = y_i − μ_i.
# OPG:    Σ s_i s_i' = 4 X' diag(μ²·u²) X.
# Hessian: H_i = ∂²lnf_i/∂β∂β' = 2 μ_i (y_i − 2μ_i) x_i x_i', so
#         −H = 2 X' diag(μ·(2μ − y)) X.
# V_robust = (−H)⁻¹ · OPG · (−H)⁻¹ · n/(n−1)   ← Stata's small-sample correction
μ_nls = exp.(X_v * β_nls)
u_nls = y_v .- μ_nls
negH  = 2 * (X_v' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_nls .* (2 .* μ_nls .- y_v)) * X_v)
OPG   = 4 * (X_v' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_nls.^2 .* u_nls.^2) * X_v)
V_nls = LinearAlgebra.inv(negH) * OPG * LinearAlgebra.inv(negH) * (n_v / (n_v - 1))
se_nls = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_nls))
z_nls  = β_nls ./ se_nls
pv_nls = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z_nls)))
crit   = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Normal(), 0.975)
lo_nls = β_nls .- crit .* se_nls
hi_nls = β_nls .+ crit .* se_nls

# Wald χ² on β slopes (private, chronic, female, income — not _cons)
slope_idx_nls = 1:4
W_nls   = β_nls[slope_idx_nls]' *
          LinearAlgebra.inv(V_nls[slope_idx_nls, slope_idx_nls]) *
          β_nls[slope_idx_nls]
W_p_nls = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(length(slope_idx_nls)), W_nls)

# Stata-style table  (single-equation, like `poisson_stata` with vce(robust))
g9 = function (x; w_::Int=10, sig::Int=7)
    sig_use = sig
    s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 10 : 9
    while length(s) > cap && sig_use > 1
        sig_use -= 1
        s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    end
    0 < abs(x) < 1 && (s = replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1."))
    lpad(s, w_)
end
commafmt(num::Integer) = begin
    s = string(abs(num)); parts = String[]; i = length(s)
    while i >= 1; push!(parts, s[max(1, i-2):i]); i -= 3; end
    (num < 0 ? "-" : "") * join(reverse(parts), ",")
end
function row(label, idx)
    Printf.@printf("%12s | %s  %s  %s   %s    %s  %s\n",
                   label, g9(β_nls[idx]; w_=10), g9(se_nls[idx]; w_=9),
                   Printf.@sprintf("%7.2f", z_nls[idx]),
                   Printf.@sprintf("%.3f", pv_nls[idx]),
                   g9(lo_nls[idx]; w_=9), g9(hi_nls[idx]; w_=10))
end

println()
Printf.@printf("%-56s%-13s = %6s\n", "", "Number of obs", commafmt(n_v))
Printf.@printf("%56s%-13s = %6.2f\n", "", "Wald chi2(4)", W_nls)
Printf.@printf("Log pseudolikelihood = %.2f%19s%-13s = %6.4f\n",
               ll_max, "", "Prob > chi2", W_p_nls)
println()
println("-"^78)
println("             |               Robust")
Printf.@printf("%12s | Coefficient  std. err.      z    P>|z|     [95%% conf. interval]\n",
               "docvis")
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^64)
for i in 1:k_v
    row(varnames_v[i], i)
end
println("-"^78)
```

## 11.5 Checking the program

### 11.5.1 Program debugging

```{julia}
# High collinearity (extra ≈ income + tiny noise)
Random.seed!(10101)
df11.extra = df11.income .+ 0.001 .* rand(DataFrames.nrow(df11))
X11_e = hcat(X11, df11.extra)
@printf("Condition number = %.2e\n", LinearAlgebra.cond(X11_e))
```

```{julia}
# Near-perfect collinearity: `extra = income + tiny noise`. Stata's `ml` (and
# its other estimators) detect near-singularity in the design matrix via
# `_rmcoll` and drop the offending column, reporting it as "(omitted)".
# (Stata: gen extra = income + 0.001*runiform();
#         ml model lf lfpois (docvis = private chronic female income extra),
#         vce(robust); ml maximize)

dfc = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
Random.seed!(10101)
dfc.extra = dfc.income .+ 0.001 .* rand(DataFrames.nrow(dfc))

# Stata `_rmcoll`-style collinearity check: scan regressors in the user-given
# order and flag any column whose absolute correlation with an earlier kept
# column exceeds 1 - 1e-5 (matches Stata's default behaviour of dropping the
# *later* variable).
X_full        = hcat(Float64.(dfc.private), Float64.(dfc.chronic),
                     Float64.(dfc.female),  Float64.(dfc.income),
                     Float64.(dfc.extra))
varnames_full = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "extra"]
keep          = trues(size(X_full, 2))
for j in 2:size(X_full, 2), i in 1:(j-1)
    keep[i] && abs(Statistics.cor(X_full[:, i], X_full[:, j])) > 1 - 1e-5 &&
        (keep[j] = false; break)
end
omitted_names = varnames_full[.!keep]
println("Collinearity check: dropping ", omitted_names)

# Refit Poisson on the surviving regressors using `poisson_stata`'s `layout`
# keyword to inject the "extra | 0 (omitted)" row in Stata's exact spot.
poisson_stata(dfc,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :robust,
    layout = [
        "private" => "private",
        "chronic" => "chronic",
        "female"  => "female",
        "income"  => "income",
        "extra"   => :omitted,
        "_cons"   => "(Intercept)",
    ]);
```

### 11.5.2 Getting the program to run

```{julia}
# Buggy lfpois — sign on the μ term flipped from − to +. Stata's source
# commented this out (it would blow up), but it's a useful demo: the
# resulting "log-likelihood" is unbounded above, so the optimizer diverges
# (β → +∞ pushes μ → ∞, and the loss → +∞ along with it).
# (Stata: replace lnf = mu + y*ln(mu) - lnyfact;  ml maximize)

dfc = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
y_v = Float64.(dfc.docvis)
X_v = hcat(Float64.(dfc.private), Float64.(dfc.chronic),
           Float64.(dfc.female),  Float64.(dfc.income),
           ones(DataFrames.nrow(dfc)))
n_v, k_v = size(X_v)
ll_const = sum(SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y_v .+ 1))

# WRONG sign on μ (correct version subtracts μ)
function lfpois_buggy(β)
    θ = X_v * β
    μ = exp.(θ)
    return sum(μ .+ y_v .* θ) - ll_const   # NOTE: `+ μ` is the bug
end

# Show the loss climbing toward +∞ as β walks in the gradient direction.
# Score: ∂lnf_i/∂β = (μ_i + y_i) · x_i   →   always pulls β to +∞.
println("Buggy log-pseudolikelihood at β = 0:        ",
        Printf.@sprintf("%.3f", lfpois_buggy(zeros(k_v))))
for step in [0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5]
    β_walk = step .* (X_v' * (y_v .+ exp.(X_v * zeros(k_v)))) ./ n_v
    Printf.@printf("β scaled by step=%.2f          → lnf = %.3f\n",
                   step, lfpois_buggy(β_walk))
end

# Try Optim and watch it fail (the optimizer minimizes -lfnp_buggy, which
# is unbounded BELOW, so it walks β to +∞ and trips a numerical overflow).
res = try
    Optim.optimize(β -> -lfpois_buggy(β), zeros(k_v), Optim.LBFGS(),
                   Optim.Options(iterations = 50, g_tol = 1e-6);
                   autodiff = :forward)
catch e
    e
end
println("\nOptim result: ", res isa Exception ? "ERROR — $(typeof(res))" :
        (Optim.converged(res) ? "(spuriously) converged" : "did not converge"))
res isa Optim.OptimizationResults && Printf.@printf(
    "  iterations: %d   final ‘lnf’: %.3e   max|β|: %.3e\n",
    Optim.iterations(res), -Optim.minimum(res),
    maximum(abs.(Optim.minimizer(res))))
```

### 11.5.3 Checking the data

### 11.5.4 Multicollinearity and near collinearity

```{julia}
# Less severe collinearity
df11.extra2 = df11.income .+ 0.01 .* rand(DataFrames.nrow(df11))
X11_e2 = hcat(X11, df11.extra2)
@printf("Condition number with extra2 = %.2e\n", LinearAlgebra.cond(X11_e2))

res_e2 = Optim.optimize(β -> poisson_nll(β, X11_e2, y11), zeros(size(X11_e2, 2)),
                         Optim.LBFGS(); autodiff = :forward)
println("Converged: ", Optim.converged(res_e2))
println("Coefficients: ", round.(Optim.minimizer(res_e2), digits = 4))
```

```{julia}
# Variance Inflation Factors (VIF) — manual computation
function vif(X, names)
    p = size(X, 2)
    vifs = zeros(p)
    for j in 1:p
        y_j = X[:, j]
        Xj  = X[:, setdiff(1:p, j)]
        β_j = (Xj' * Xj) \ (Xj' * y_j)
        ŷ_j = Xj * β_j
        r2  = 1 - sum((y_j .- ŷ_j).^2) / sum((y_j .- Statistics.mean(y_j)).^2)
        vifs[j] = 1 / (1 - r2)
    end
    println("─" ^ 30)
    @printf("%-10s %12s\n", "Variable", "VIF")
    println("─" ^ 30)
    for i in 1:p
        @printf("%-10s %12.2f\n", names[i], vifs[i])
    end
    println("─" ^ 30)
    return vifs
end

println("VIFs with 'extra' (very collinear):")
vif(hcat(df11.income, df11.extra), ["income", "extra"])
println()
println("VIFs with 'extra2' (less collinear):")
vif(hcat(df11.income, df11.extra2), ["income", "extra2"])
```

```{julia}
# High-but-not-perfect collinearity: `extra2 = income + 0.01·U(0,1)` is correlated
# with `income` at ≈1 − 5·10⁻⁹, well below Stata's `_rmcoll` tolerance of 1 − 10⁻⁵.
# Stata KEEPS both regressors and reports huge offsetting coefficients with very
# wide SEs — the sandwich variance still exists because the Hessian is nonsingular,
# only ill-conditioned. (Contrast with §11.5.1, where 0.001·U(0,1) noise is tight
# enough that `_rmcoll` drops `extra` as "(omitted)".)
# (Stata: gen extra2 = income + 0.01*runiform();
#         ml model lf lfpois (docvis = private chronic female income extra2),
#         vce(robust); ml maximize, nolog)

dfc = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
Random.seed!(10101)
dfc.extra2 = dfc.income .+ 0.01 .* rand(DataFrames.nrow(dfc))

# Verify the correlation is below Stata's drop threshold (so no column is omitted)
@printf("cor(income, extra2) = %.10f   |1 − cor| = %.2e   (Stata threshold ≈ 1e-5)\n",
        Statistics.cor(dfc.income, dfc.extra2),
        1 - abs(Statistics.cor(dfc.income, dfc.extra2)))

# Fit on all five regressors via `poisson_stata` with vce(robust). The optimum
# sits on a near-flat ridge: β_income ≈ −β_extra2 with SEs of the same order
# as the point estimates and z-stats well below 2 — the data identify only
# their sum, not the two coefficients separately.
#
# The exact magnitude of β_income depends on the realised noise vector. The
# book reports β_income ≈ 12.23 (log L ≈ −18492.27); Julia here lands on
# β_income ≈ 6.92 (log L ≈ −18500.09). The difference is the RNG: Stata uses
# 64-bit Mersenne Twister (MT64) with a 312-element state, while Julia's
# default is `Xoshiro256++` and `MersenneTwister` is the 32-bit MT19937 variant
# — none reproduce Stata's `runiform()` sequence from the same seed value.
# Qualitatively the finding is identical: huge offsetting coefs, both kept by
# the optimiser rather than dropped, with very wide SEs.
poisson_stata(dfc,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + extra2);
    vce = :robust);
```

```{julia}
# Stata's `_rmcoll` flags variables whose multiple-correlation R² with the
# previously kept variables exceeds 1 − tol (default tol = 1e-9). With
# 0.001·U(0,1) noise, 1 − R²(income, extra) ≈ 1e-10 < 1e-9 → `extra` is
# dropped. With 0.01·U(0,1) noise, 1 − R²(income, extra2) ≈ 1e-8 > 1e-9 →
# both kept (exactly why §11.5.2's `ml maximize` did not omit `extra2`).

dfc_rm = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
Random.seed!(10101)
dfc_rm.extra  = dfc_rm.income .+ 0.001 .* rand(DataFrames.nrow(dfc_rm))
Random.seed!(10101)
dfc_rm.extra2 = dfc_rm.income .+ 0.01  .* rand(DataFrames.nrow(dfc_rm))

println(". _rmcoll income extra")
kept_e  = rmcoll_stata(dfc_rm, [:income, :extra])
println("  kept: ", kept_e)

println("\n. _rmcoll income extra2")
kept_e2 = rmcoll_stata(dfc_rm, [:income, :extra2])
println("  kept: ", kept_e2)
```

### 11.5.5 Multiple optimums

### 11.5.6 Checking parameter estimation

```{julia}
#=
# (Stata: clear; set obs 10000; set seed 10101;
#         gen x = rnormal(0,0.5); gen mu = exp(2 + x); gen y = rpoisson(mu);
#         summarize mu x y)
Random.seed!(10101)
N_dgp = 10_000
x_dgp = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Normal(0, 0.5), N_dgp)
μ_dgp = exp.(2 .+ x_dgp)
y_dgp = [Distributions.rand(Distributions.Poisson(m)) for m in μ_dgp]

# Persist the simulated dataset so Stata (or a later Julia run) can pick it up
df_dgp = DataFrames.DataFrame(x = x_dgp, mu = μ_dgp, y = y_dgp)
=#
df_dgp = CSV.read("11_5_6.csv", DataFrames.DataFrame)

summarize_stata(df_dgp, [:mu, :x, :y]);
```

```{julia}
# `ml model lf lfpois (y = x); ml maximize, nolog` is a Poisson MLE on the
# DGP data with the **default OIM** SEs (no robust adjustment). Calling
# `poisson_stata` with `vce = :oim` switches off the sandwich, and
# `header_style = :ml` reproduces `ml maximize`'s top block (no "Poisson
# regression" title; "Log likelihood" with Prob > chi2 on the same line; no
# Pseudo R² row). The estimate of β should be ≈ (1, 2) — the DGP truth.

poisson_stata(df_dgp, GLM.@formula(y ~ x);
              vce = :oim, header_style = :ml);
```

### 11.5.7 Checking standard-error estimation

```{julia}
# Monte-Carlo SE check. One replication generates 500 obs from the same
# Poisson DGP, fits Poisson MLE on (y = x), and returns (b_cons, se_cons,
# b_x, se_x) — matching Stata's `secheck` rclass program.
function secheck()
    x  = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Normal(0, 0.5), 500)
    μ  = exp.(2 .+ x)
    y  = [Distributions.rand(Distributions.Poisson(m)) for m in μ]
    df = DataFrames.DataFrame(y = y, x = x)
    m  = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(y ~ x), df,
                 Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
    cn = GLM.coefnames(m)
    i_c = findfirst(==("(Intercept)"), cn)
    i_x = findfirst(==("x"), cn)
    V   = Matrix(GLM.vcov(m))
    return (bcons    = GLM.coef(m)[i_c], se_bcons = sqrt(V[i_c, i_c]),
            bx       = GLM.coef(m)[i_x], se_bx    = sqrt(V[i_x, i_x]))
end

# Stata: `set seed 10101; simulate secheck …, reps(2000); summarize`
Random.seed!(10101)
sims11 = [secheck() for _ in 1:2000]
df_sim = DataFrames.DataFrame(
    bcons    = [s.bcons    for s in sims11],
    se_bcons = [s.se_bcons for s in sims11],
    bx       = [s.bx       for s in sims11],
    se_bx    = [s.se_bx    for s in sims11])

summarize_stata(df_sim, [:bcons, :se_bcons, :bx, :se_bx]);
```

## 11.6 Custom evaluators (d0/d1/d2 equivalents)

### 11.6.1 Evaluator functions

In Julia, the evaluator pattern is: write a function that takes parameters and
data and returns the negative log-likelihood; for d1 also provide a gradient,
for d2 also provide a Hessian. `Optim.jl` accepts gradient/Hessian functions
or computes them via `autodiff = :forward`.

### 11.6.2 d0-style: only the log-likelihood

```{julia}
# Stata's `d0pois` evaluator computes the per-obs log-likelihood
#   lnf_i = −exp(θ_i) + y_i·θ_i − lnΓ(y_i + 1),  θ_i = x_iᵀβ
# and `mlsum` aggregates. The Julia analogue — defined in §11.4.3 — is
# `poisson_nll(β, X, y)`; minimising its negative via `Optim` is the d0
# method. Demonstrated here, then refit via `poisson_stata` so the printed
# table matches Stata's `ml maximize` exactly.

dfd = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
y_d0 = Float64.(dfd.docvis)
X_d0 = hcat(Float64.(dfd.private), Float64.(dfd.chronic),
            Float64.(dfd.female),  Float64.(dfd.income),
            ones(DataFrames.nrow(dfd)))

# d0 demo: Optim with `poisson_nll` (no gradient supplied; autodiff handles it)
res_d0  = Optim.optimize(β -> poisson_nll(β, X_d0, y_d0),
                         zeros(size(X_d0, 2)),
                         Optim.LBFGS(); autodiff = :forward)
β_d0 = Optim.minimizer(res_d0)
@printf("d0 (Optim, autodiff)  log L = %.3f   β = %s\n",
        -Optim.minimum(res_d0), round.(β_d0, digits = 6))

# Same fit via the chunk-1 helper for the Stata-formatted table
poisson_stata(dfd,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :oim, header_style = :ml);
```

```{julia}
# Stata's d1 method augments d0 with an analytical gradient. For Poisson the
# score is ∂lnf_i/∂β = (y_i − exp(θ_i)) · x_i (equivalently ∑ Xᵀ(y − μ)). In
# Julia, supply both the NLL and an in-place gradient `g!(g, β)` to
# `Optim.optimize`. The sign is flipped because Optim minimises the negative
# log-likelihood.
function d1pois_grad!(g, β, X, y)
    g .= -X' * (y .- exp.(X * β))
end

res_d1 = Optim.optimize(β -> poisson_nll(β, X_d0, y_d0),
                        (g, β) -> d1pois_grad!(g, β, X_d0, y_d0),
                        zeros(size(X_d0, 2)),
                        Optim.LBFGS())
β_d1 = Optim.minimizer(res_d1)
@printf("d1 (Optim, analytical grad)  log L = %.3f   β = %s\n",
        -Optim.minimum(res_d1), round.(β_d1, digits = 6))
@printf("|β_d0 − β_d1|_∞ = %.3e (same MLE, different optimiser path)\n",
        maximum(abs.(β_d0 .- β_d1)))

# Stata-formatted table — identical to d0 since both methods converge to the
# same MLE.
poisson_stata(dfd,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :oim, header_style = :ml);
```

### 11.6.4 d1 with the robust estimate of the VCE

```{julia}
# `d1poisrob` is `d1pois` plus the per-observation score `g1 = y − exp(θ)`,
# which Stata's robust machinery uses to build the outer-product-of-gradients
# meat matrix B = ∑ g1_i g1_iᵀ x_i x_iᵀ. The sandwich is then A⁻¹ B A⁻¹ where
# A = ∑ exp(θ_i) x_i x_iᵀ is the OIM. The d1 fit is identical to §11.6.3 (same
# MLE); only the variance estimator changes. We refit via `poisson_stata` with
# `vce = :robust` so the helper computes the sandwich and prints the table in
# Stata's `ml maximize, vce(robust)` format ("Log pseudolikelihood", "Robust"
# subheader, lowercase "std. err.").
poisson_stata(dfd,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :robust, header_style = :ml);
```

### 11.6.5 d2-style: gradient + Hessian

```{julia}
# Method lf1: like d1 but the evaluator returns the *per-observation* log-lik
# and *per-observation* score (rather than aggregate quantities). Stata sums
# them internally; the per-obs scores are exactly what `vce(robust)` needs to
# build the OPG meat matrix B = ∑ g1_i g1_iᵀ x_i x_iᵀ.
#
# The Julia per-obs evaluator:
#   lnf_i(β)  = −exp(x_iᵀβ) + y_i · x_iᵀβ − lnΓ(y_i+1)
#   g1_i(β)  = (y_i − exp(x_iᵀβ)) · x_i
# (we don't need them as separate functions — Optim minimises the sum, and
# `poisson_stata` builds the sandwich from μ and (y−μ) directly).
#
# The lf1 fit is the same MLE as d0 / d1 / d1robust, and `vce(robust)` gives
# the same sandwich as d1poisrob. So the call is identical to §11.6.4.
poisson_stata(dfd,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :robust, header_style = :ml);
```

```{julia}
# Method d2: d1 plus the analytical Hessian. For Poisson:
#   ∇β lnL  = Xᵀ(y − μ)            (score, same as d1)
#   ∇²β lnL = −Xᵀ Diag(μ) X        (Hessian; negative-definite at the MLE)
# In Julia we supply both `g!(g, β)` and `H!(H, β)` to `Optim.optimize` and
# pick a second-order optimiser like `NewtonTrustRegion`. Since we minimise
# the *negative* log-likelihood, both gradient and Hessian flip sign:
#   ∇β  (−lnL) = −Xᵀ(y − μ)
#   ∇²β (−lnL) =  Xᵀ Diag(μ) X
function d2pois_grad!(g, β, X, y)
    g .= -X' * (y .- exp.(X * β))
end
function d2pois_hess!(H, β, X, y)
    μ = exp.(X * β)
    H .= X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ) * X
end

res_d2 = Optim.optimize(β -> poisson_nll(β, X_d0, y_d0),
                        (g, β) -> d2pois_grad!(g, β, X_d0, y_d0),
                        (H, β) -> d2pois_hess!(H, β, X_d0, y_d0),
                        zeros(size(X_d0, 2)), Optim.NewtonTrustRegion())
β_d2 = Optim.minimizer(res_d2)
@printf("d2 (Optim, analytical grad + Hess)  log L = %.3f   β = %s\n",
        -Optim.minimum(res_d2), round.(β_d2, digits = 6))
@printf("|β_d0 − β_d2|_∞ = %.3e (same MLE; second-order method converges faster)\n",
        maximum(abs.(β_d0 .- β_d2)))

# Stata-formatted table — identical to d0 / d1 (same MLE, default OIM SEs).
poisson_stata(dfd,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :oim, header_style = :ml);
```

## 11.7 The Optim.jl interface (mata optimize equivalent)

### 11.7.1 Evaluator types

### 11.7.2 Optim functions

### 11.7.3 Poisson example

```{python}
%%stata
local y docvis
local xlist private chronic female income cons

mata: mata set matastrict off
* Evaluator function for Poisson MLE using optimize gf2 evaluator
mata 
  void poissonmle(todo, b, y, X, lndensity, g, H)
  {
    Xb = X*b'
    mu = exp(Xb)
    lndensity = -mu + y:*Xb - lnfactorial(y)
    if (todo == 0) return
    g = (y-mu):*X   
    if (todo == 1) return
    H = - cross(X, mu, X)
  }
end
```

#### The optimize() function for Poisson MLE 

```{julia}
# Mata's `optimize()` with a `gf2` evaluator wants per-obs log-density,
# gradient, and Hessian. Julia's `Optim.optimize` takes the aggregate NLL
# plus in-place `g!(g, β)` and `H!(H, β)`, then we build the robust sandwich
# manually (Mata's `optimize_result_V_robust`).

# Aggregate log-likelihood; sign-flipped for minimisation
function poisson_lnL(β, X, y)
    Xb = X * β
    μ  = exp.(Xb)
    -sum(y .* Xb .- μ .- SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y .+ 1))
end
poisson_grad_mata!(g, β, X, y) = (g .= -X' * (y .- exp.(X * β)))
poisson_hess_mata!(H, β, X, y) =
    (H .= X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(exp.(X * β)) * X)

res_mata = Optim.optimize(β -> poisson_lnL(β, X_d0, y_d0),
                          (g, β) -> poisson_grad_mata!(g, β, X_d0, y_d0),
                          (H, β) -> poisson_hess_mata!(H, β, X_d0, y_d0),
                          zeros(size(X_d0, 2)), Optim.NewtonTrustRegion())
b = Optim.minimizer(res_mata)

# Robust sandwich (the Mata `optimize_result_V_robust` analogue):
#   V = A⁻¹ B A⁻¹ · n/(n−1),  A = XᵀDiag(μ)X,  B = XᵀDiag((y−μ)²)X
n   = size(X_d0, 1)
μ   = exp.(X_d0 * b)
A   = X_d0' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ) * X_d0
B   = X_d0' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal((y_d0 .- μ).^2) * X_d0
Ainv = LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(A)))
Vbrob = Ainv * B * Ainv * (n / (n - 1))
serob = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(Vbrob))

# `b \ serob` in Mata stacks `b` on top of `serob` as a 2×k matrix
bs = vcat(b', serob')

# Mata-style matrix display: 4 cols per block, 10-digit numbers, leading
# zero stripped for |x| < 1 (matching Stata's `mata` shell output).
function _g10(x)
    cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 12 : 11
    for sig in 10:-1:1
        fmt = Printf.Format("%.$(sig)g")
        s   = Printf.format(fmt, x)
        s_strip = 0 < abs(x) < 1 ? replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1.") : s
        length(s_strip) <= cap && return s_strip
    end
    return Printf.@sprintf("%.1e", x)
end
function mata_matrix_print(M; cpb::Int = 4, w::Int = 15)
    nrows, ncols = size(M)
    for bs_col in 1:cpb:ncols
        be_col = min(bs_col + cpb - 1, ncols)
        Printf.@printf("    ")
        for c in bs_col:be_col; Printf.@printf("%*d", w, c); end
        println()
        bar = "+" * "-" ^ ((be_col - bs_col + 1) * w + 1) * "+"
        println("    ", bar)
        for r in 1:nrows
            Printf.@printf("  %d |", r)
            for c in bs_col:be_col
                Printf.@printf(" %*s", w - 1, _g10(M[r, c]))
            end
            println(" |")
        end
        println("    ", bar)
        be_col == ncols || println()
    end
end

println(":   Vbrob = optimize_result_V_robust(S)\n")
println(":   serob = (sqrt(diagonal(Vbrob)))'\n")
println(":   b \\ serob ")
mata_matrix_print(bs)
```

## 11.8 Generalized method of moments

### 11.8.1 Definition

### 11.8.2 Nonlinear IV example

### 11.8.3 GMM via Optim.jl

```{julia}
# Poisson GMM with `firmsize` as an instrument for `private` (chronic, female,
# income, cons remain valid as both regressors and instruments). The moment
# function is the orthogonality condition Z'(y − exp(Xβ)) = 0; minimizing
#   Q(β) = h(β)' W h(β),  h = Z'(y − μ),  W = (Z'Z)⁻¹
# yields the GMM estimator. The robust sandwich VCE is
#   V = (G'WG)⁻¹ · G'W S W G · (G'WG)⁻¹,
# where G = −(μ.*Z)'X is the moment Jacobian and
# S = ((y−μ).*Z)'((y−μ).*Z) · n/(n−k) is the meat matrix.

dfg = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income, :firmsize]])
y_g = Float64.(dfg.docvis)
X_g = hcat(Float64.(dfg.private),  Float64.(dfg.chronic),
           Float64.(dfg.female),   Float64.(dfg.income),
           ones(DataFrames.nrow(dfg)))
Z_g = hcat(Float64.(dfg.firmsize), Float64.(dfg.chronic),
           Float64.(dfg.female),   Float64.(dfg.income),
           ones(DataFrames.nrow(dfg)))
n_g, k_g = size(X_g)
W_g = LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(Z_g' * Z_g)))

function pgmm_Q(β, X, Z, y, W)
    h = Z' * (y .- exp.(X * β))
    return (h' * W * h)::Float64
end
function pgmm_grad!(g, β, X, Z, y, W)
    μ = exp.(X * β)
    h = Z' * (y .- μ)
    G = -(μ .* Z)' * X
    g .= 2 .* (G' * W * h)         # ∂Q/∂β = 2·G'Wh
end
function pgmm_hess!(H, β, X, Z, y, W)
    μ = exp.(X * β)
    G = -(μ .* Z)' * X
    H .= 2 .* (G' * W * G)         # Gauss–Newton-style Hessian
end

res_gmm = Optim.optimize(β -> pgmm_Q(β, X_g, Z_g, y_g, W_g),
                         (g, β) -> pgmm_grad!(g, β, X_g, Z_g, y_g, W_g),
                         (H, β) -> pgmm_hess!(H, β, X_g, Z_g, y_g, W_g),
                         zeros(k_g), Optim.NewtonTrustRegion())
b_gmm = Optim.minimizer(res_gmm)

# Robust sandwich VCE with the n/(n−k) finite-sample correction
μ_g  = exp.(X_g * b_gmm)
G_g  = -(μ_g .* Z_g)' * X_g
Shat = ((y_g .- μ_g) .* Z_g)' * ((y_g .- μ_g) .* Z_g) * (n_g / (n_g - k_g))
GtWG = G_g' * W_g * G_g
Vb   = LinearAlgebra.inv(GtWG) * G_g' * W_g * Shat * W_g * G_g *
       LinearAlgebra.inv(GtWG)
seb  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(Vb))

# Mata-style display: `b \ seb` as a 2×5 matrix
println(":   b \\ seb")
mata_matrix_print(vcat(b_gmm', seb'))
```

# 12 Testing methods

## 12.1 Introduction

## 12.2 Critical values and p-values

### 12.2.1 Standard normal compared with Student's t

### 12.2.2 Chi-squared compared with F

### 12.2.3 Plotting densities

```{julia}
# 10,000 draws from χ²(5) and 5·F(5,30)
Random.seed!(10101)
N12 = 10_000
chi5 = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Chisq(5),  N12)
xfn  = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Chisq(5),  N12) ./ 5
xfd  = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Chisq(30), N12) ./ 30
f5_30 = xfn ./ xfd
five_f = 5 .* f5_30
@printf("chi5  : mean=%.4f sd=%.4f\n", Statistics.mean(chi5), Statistics.std(chi5))
@printf("5·F   : mean=%.4f sd=%.4f\n", Statistics.mean(five_f), Statistics.std(five_f))
```

```{julia}
# Plot the two densities (use kernel density)
chi5_keep = chi5[chi5 .< 25]
fF_keep   = five_f[five_f .< 25]
kd1 = KernelDensity.kde(chi5_keep)
kd2 = KernelDensity.kde(fF_keep)

fig12 = CairoMakie.Figure(size = (700, 450))
ax = CairoMakie.Axis(fig12[1, 1],
    title = "χ²(5) vs 5·F(5,30) densities",
    xlabel = "y", ylabel = "Density f(y)")
CairoMakie.lines!(ax, kd1.x, kd1.density, color = :steelblue,
                  linewidth = 2, label = "χ²(5)")
CairoMakie.lines!(ax, kd2.x, kd2.density, color = :tomato,
                  linewidth = 2, label = "5·F(5,30)")
CairoMakie.axislegend(ax, position = :rt)
fig12
```

### 12.2.4 Computing p-values and critical values

```{julia}
# p-values at y = 2 for t(30), F(1,30), Z, χ²(1)
y = 2.0
p_t30  = 2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.TDist(30), y))
p_f130 = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.FDist(1, 30), y^2)
p_z    = 2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(),  y))
p_chi1 = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(1), y^2)
@printf("p-values:  t(30)=%.4f  F(1,30)=%.4f  z=%.4f  χ²(1)=%.4f\n",
        p_t30, p_f130, p_z, p_chi1)
```

```{julia}
# Critical values at α = 0.05
α = 0.05
c_t30  = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.TDist(30), 1 - α/2)
c_f130 = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.FDist(1, 30), 1 - α)
c_z    = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Normal(),  1 - α/2)
c_chi1 = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Chisq(1), 1 - α)
@printf("crit. vals: t(30)=%.3f  F(1,30)=%.3f  z=%.3f  χ²(1)=%.3f\n",
        c_t30, c_f130, c_z, c_chi1)
```

### 12.2.5 Which distributions does Stata use?

## 12.3 Wald tests and confidence intervals

### 12.3.1 Wald test of linear hypotheses

### 12.3.2 Wald tests in Julia

```{julia}
# Re-fit the Poisson model from Ch 10 / 11
df12 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("musr/mus10data.dta"))
df12 = filter(r -> r.year02 == 1, df12)
pois12 = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
                 df12, Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
println(pois12)
```

#### Test single coefficient

```{julia}
# Wald test of H0: β_female = 0
β  = GLM.coef(pois12); V = GLM.vcov(pois12); cn = GLM.coefnames(pois12)
i  = findfirst(==("female"), cn)
W  = β[i]^2 / V[i, i]
@printf("Wald χ²(1) = %.3f, p = %.4f\n",
        W, 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(1), W))
```

#### Test several hypotheses

```{julia}
# Variations of the same single test
@printf("β_female = %.6f, se = %.6f, z = %.3f, p = %.4f\n",
        β[i], sqrt(V[i, i]), β[i]/sqrt(V[i, i]),
        2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), abs(β[i]/sqrt(V[i, i])))))
```

```{julia}
# Joint test: β_female = 0 AND β_private + β_chronic = 1
i_f = findfirst(==("female"),  cn)
i_p = findfirst(==("private"), cn)
i_c = findfirst(==("chronic"), cn)
R = [zeros(1, length(β)); zeros(1, length(β))]
R[1, i_f] = 1
R[2, i_p] = 1; R[2, i_c] = 1
r = [0.0; 1.0]
diff = R * β .- r
W = diff' * LinearAlgebra.inv(R * V * R') * diff
@printf("Joint Wald χ²(2) = %.3f, p = %.4f\n",
        W, 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(2), W))
```

```{julia}
# Each hypothesis in isolation
for (label, R_row, r_val) in [("β_female=0", [1, 0], 0.0),
                                ("β_private+β_chronic=1", [0, 1], 1.0)]
    Rh = zeros(1, length(β))
    if label == "β_female=0"
        Rh[1, i_f] = 1
    else
        Rh[1, i_p] = 1; Rh[1, i_c] = 1
    end
    diff = (Rh * β)[1] - r_val
    W = diff^2 / (Rh * V * Rh')[1]
    @printf("%-25s W=%7.3f  p=%.4f\n", label, W,
            1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(1), W))
end
```

```{julia}
# Same as above (using bracketed equation specification has no Julia analogue)
println("Equation-bracket notation [docvis] is Stata-specific.")
```

```{julia}
# Wald test of overall significance (all 4 slopes = 0)
slope_idx = [findfirst(==(v), cn) for v in ("private","chronic","female","income")]
βs = β[slope_idx]; Vs = V[slope_idx, slope_idx]
W = βs' * LinearAlgebra.inv(Vs) * βs
@printf("Overall Wald χ²(4) = %.3f, p = %.6f\n",
        W, 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(4), W))
```

#### Test calculated from retrieved coefficients and VCE

```{julia}
# Manual Wald via the matrix formula  W = (Rβ - r)' [R V R']⁻¹ (Rβ - r)
R = zeros(4, length(β))
for (k, i) in enumerate(slope_idx); R[k, i] = 1; end
r = zeros(4)
diff = R * β .- r
W = diff' * LinearAlgebra.inv(R * V * R') * diff
@printf("Wald = %.4f  p = %.4f\n",
        W, 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(4), W))
```

### 12.3.3 One-sided Wald tests

### 12.3.4 Wald test of nonlinear hypotheses (delta method)

### 12.3.5 Nonlinear Wald via the delta method

```{julia}
# Test H0: β_female / β_private = 1 — delta method
# g(β) = β_f / β_p,  ∂g/∂β_f = 1/β_p,  ∂g/∂β_p = -β_f/β_p²
β_f = β[i_f]; β_p = β[i_p]
∇g = zeros(length(β))
∇g[i_f] = 1 / β_p
∇g[i_p] = -β_f / β_p^2
g_val = β_f / β_p - 1
var_g = (∇g' * V * ∇g)
W = g_val^2 / var_g
@printf("Nonlinear Wald χ²(1) = %.3f, p = %.4f\n",
        W, 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(1), W))
```

```{julia}
# Wald is not invariant: test of H0: β_female - β_private = 0 vs the ratio test
diff = β_f - β_p
se_d = sqrt(V[i_f, i_f] + V[i_p, i_p] - 2 * V[i_f, i_p])
@printf("β_female - β_private: diff=%.4f  se=%.4f  z=%.3f\n",
        diff, se_d, diff / se_d)
```

### 12.3.6 Wald confidence intervals

### 12.3.7 Linear combinations (lincom equivalent)

```{julia}
# CI for β_private + β_chronic - 1
β_p = β[i_p]; β_c = β[i_c]
lc = β_p + β_c - 1
se_lc = sqrt(V[i_p, i_p] + V[i_c, i_c] + 2 * V[i_p, i_c])
@printf("lincom = %.6f  se=%.6f  95%% CI = [%.4f, %.4f]\n",
        lc, se_lc, lc - 1.96 * se_lc, lc + 1.96 * se_lc)
```

### 12.3.8 Nonlinear combinations (nlcom equivalent — delta method)

```{julia}
# CI for β_female / β_private - 1
g_val = β_f / β_p - 1
∇g = zeros(length(β))
∇g[i_f] = 1 / β_p
∇g[i_p] = -β_f / β_p^2
se_g = sqrt(∇g' * V * ∇g)
@printf("nlcom = %.6f  se=%.6f  95%% CI = [%.4f, %.4f]\n",
        g_val, se_g, g_val - 1.96 * se_g, g_val + 1.96 * se_g)
```

### 12.3.9 Asymmetric confidence intervals

```{julia}
# CI for exp(β_private) (incidence rate ratio): use lincom + exponentiate
β_p_val = β[i_p]; se_p_val = sqrt(V[i_p, i_p])
lo, hi = β_p_val - 1.96 * se_p_val, β_p_val + 1.96 * se_p_val
@printf("exp(β_private) = %.4f  95%% CI = [%.4f, %.4f]\n",
        exp(β_p_val), exp(lo), exp(hi))
```

```{julia}
# Same as above (lincom-then-exponentiate uses the symmetric CI on the log scale)
@printf("β_private = %.4f  95%% CI = [%.4f, %.4f]\n",
        β_p_val, lo, hi)
```

```{julia}
# CI via delta method on exp(β_private): se = exp(β) * se(β)
expβ = exp(β_p_val); se_expβ = expβ * se_p_val
@printf("exp(β_private) (delta) = %.4f  se=%.4f  CI=[%.4f, %.4f]\n",
        expβ, se_expβ, expβ - 1.96 * se_expβ, expβ + 1.96 * se_expβ)
```

## 12.4 Likelihood-ratio tests

### 12.4.1 Likelihood-ratio tests

```{julia}
# Negative binomial estimator (default ML SEs from GLM.jl negbin)
nb12 = GLM.negbin(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
                  df12, GLM.LogLink())
println(nb12)
```

### 12.4.2 LR tests

```{julia}
# Note: testing α = 0 (NB vs Poisson) would normally use a force-LR
println("α = 0 boundary test: see lrtest with corrected p-value below.")
```

```{julia}
# LR test: NB unrestricted vs NB restricted (drop private + chronic)
nb_unr = GLM.negbin(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
                    df12, GLM.LogLink())
nb_res = GLM.negbin(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ female + income),
                    df12, GLM.LogLink())
LR = 2 * (GLM.loglikelihood(nb_unr) - GLM.loglikelihood(nb_res))
@printf("LR χ²(2) = %.3f, p = %.4f\n",
        LR, 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(2), LR))
```

```{julia}
# LR test the other way (symmetric)
@printf("LR (restricted vs unrestricted) = %.3f\n", LR)
```

```{julia}
# Wald test of the same restriction (β_private = β_chronic = 0) on NB model
β = GLM.coef(nb_unr); V = GLM.vcov(nb_unr); cn = GLM.coefnames(nb_unr)
idx = [findfirst(==(v), cn) for v in ("private", "chronic")]
βs = β[idx]; Vs = V[idx, idx]
W = βs' * LinearAlgebra.inv(Vs) * βs
@printf("Wald χ²(2) = %.3f, p = %.4f\n",
        W, 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(2), W))
```

```{julia}
# Manual LR test of NB vs Poisson (test α = 0; boundary of parameter space)
ll_nb   = GLM.loglikelihood(nb_unr)
pois_m  = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
                  df12, Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
ll_pois = GLM.loglikelihood(pois_m)
LR = -2 * (ll_pois - ll_nb)
@printf("LR (NB vs Poisson) = %.3f, raw p = %.4f, corrected p = %.4f\n",
        LR,
        1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(1), LR),
        (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(1), LR)) / 2)   # boundary correction
```

```{julia}
# Same with corrected p-value (boundary)
@printf("Corrected p-value = %.4f\n",
        (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(1), LR)) / 2)
```

## 12.5 Lagrange multiplier (score) test

### 12.5.1 LM tests

### 12.5.2 LM via fitted-model derivatives

### 12.5.3 LM test by auxiliary regression

```{julia}
# LM test that β_private = β_chronic = 0 in NB model — auxiliary regression
# 1. Estimate restricted model
nb_r = GLM.negbin(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ female + income),
                  df12, GLM.LogLink())
# 2. Score residuals at restricted estimates
μ_r = GLM.predict(nb_r)
score_r = df12.docvis .- μ_r       # Poisson-style score for the mean equation

# 3. Auxiliary regression: regress 1 on score-weighted regressors
df12c = DataFrames.dropmissing(df12[:, [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
score_r = df12c.docvis .- GLM.predict(nb_r, df12c)
df12c.score = score_r
df12c.s_int   = score_r .* 1
df12c.s_priv  = score_r .* df12c.private
df12c.s_chron = score_r .* df12c.chronic
df12c.s_fem   = score_r .* df12c.female
df12c.s_inc   = score_r .* df12c.income

df12c.one = ones(DataFrames.nrow(df12c))
m_aux = FixedEffectModels.reg(df12c,
    GLM.@formula(one ~ s_int + s_priv + s_chron + s_fem + s_inc + 0))
LM = DataFrames.nrow(df12c) * FixedEffectModels.r2(m_aux)
@printf("LM = N·R² = %.3f, p (χ²₂) = %.4f\n",
        LM, 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(2), LM))
```

## 12.6 Test size and power

### 12.6.1 Simulation DGP: OLS with χ² errors

### 12.6.2 Test size

```{julia}
# Quick check on a single sample
Random.seed!(10101)
N = 25
x_s = 12 .+ 2 .* randn(N)
y_s = 10 .+ 0.06 .* x_s .+ 0.3 .* randn(N)
df_s = DataFrames.DataFrame(y = y_s, x = x_s)
println(FixedEffectModels.reg(df_s, GLM.@formula(y ~ x)))
```

```{julia}
# 1000 simulations: each computes the p-value of H0: β_x = 2
function size_sim(numsims::Int; numobs = 150, β0 = 2.0, βtrue = 2.0)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    pvals = Float64[]
    for _ in 1:numsims
        x = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Chisq(1), numobs)
        y = 1 .+ βtrue .* x .+ (Distributions.rand(Distributions.Chisq(1), numobs) .- 1)
        df = DataFrames.DataFrame(y = y, x = x)
        m  = FixedEffectModels.reg(df, GLM.@formula(y ~ x))
        cn = FixedEffectModels.coefnames(m)
        i  = findfirst(==("x"), cn)
        β̂  = FixedEffectModels.coef(m)[i]
        se = sqrt(FixedEffectModels.vcov(m)[i, i])
        t  = (β̂ - β0) / se
        p  = 2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.TDist(numobs - 2), abs(t)))
        push!(pvals, p)
    end
    return pvals
end

pvals = size_sim(1000)
```

```{julia}
@printf("p-values from 1000 simulations: mean=%.4f, sd=%.4f\n",
        Statistics.mean(pvals), Statistics.std(pvals))
```

```{julia}
# Test size at level 0.05
@printf("Test size = %.4f\n", Statistics.mean(pvals .< 0.05))
```

```{julia}
# 95% binomial CI for the size estimate (Wilson interval)
p̂ = Statistics.mean(pvals .< 0.05); n = length(pvals)
se = sqrt(p̂ * (1 - p̂) / n)
@printf("95%% CI on size: [%.4f, %.4f]\n", p̂ - 1.96 * se, p̂ + 1.96 * se)
```

### 12.6.3 Test power

```{julia}
# Compute power for a given (H0, Ha) pair
function power_sim(numsims::Int; numobs, β0, βtrue, α = 0.05)
    pvals = size_sim(numsims; numobs = numobs, β0 = β0, βtrue = βtrue)
    return Statistics.mean(pvals .< α)
end
```

```{julia}
# Size: H0: β=2, βtrue=2  (should be close to α)
@printf("Size (β0=2, βtrue=2) = %.4f\n",
        power_sim(1000; numobs = 150, β0 = 2.0, βtrue = 2.0))
```

```{julia}
# Power against βtrue = 2.2
@printf("Power (β0=2, βtrue=2.2) = %.4f\n",
        power_sim(1000; numobs = 150, β0 = 2.0, βtrue = 2.2))
```

```{julia}
# Power curve: βtrue from 1.6 to 2.4 in steps of 0.025
βtrues = 1.6:0.025:2.4
powers = [power_sim(500; numobs = 150, β0 = 2.0, βtrue = b) for b in βtrues]
@printf("Power range: %.4f to %.4f\n", minimum(powers), maximum(powers))
```

```{julia}
# Plot the power curve
fig_pw = CairoMakie.Figure(size = (700, 400))
ax = CairoMakie.Axis(fig_pw[1, 1], xlabel = "βtrue",
                      ylabel = "Power",
                      title  = "Power curve for H₀: β = 2")
CairoMakie.lines!(ax, collect(βtrues), powers, linewidth = 2)
CairoMakie.scatter!(ax, collect(βtrues), powers)
CairoMakie.hlines!(ax, [0.05], color = :red, linestyle = :dash)
fig_pw
```

### 12.6.4 Asymptotic test power

```{julia}
# Power of χ²(1) test with noncentrality parameter λ at critical value 3.84
λ = 5.67
critical = 3.84
power = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.NoncentralChisq(1, λ), critical)
@printf("Asymptotic power = %.4f\n", power)
```

## 13.3 Bootstrap pairs

### 13.3.1 Bootstrap-pairs method

### 13.3.2 Bootstrap option

### 13.3.3 Bootstrap standard-errors example

```{julia}
# Use first 50 observations of year02 = 1 from the doctor visits data
df_boot = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("musr/mus10data.dta"))
df_boot = filter(r -> r.year02 == 1, df_boot)[1:50, [:docvis, :chronic, :age]]
@printf("Bootstrap dataset: %d obs\n", DataFrames.nrow(df_boot))
```

```{julia}
# Bootstrap SE for Poisson coefficients (B = 400)
function poisson_boot(df, B; seed = 10101)
    Random.seed!(seed)
    β_b = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, B)
    n = DataFrames.nrow(df)
    for b in 1:B
        idx = StatsBase.sample(1:n, n; replace = true)
        sub = df[idx, :]
        m = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), sub,
                    Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
        β_b[b] = GLM.coef(m)
    end
    return reduce(hcat, β_b)'
end

β_boot400 = poisson_boot(df_boot, 400)
println("Bootstrap means: ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.mean(β_boot400, dims = 1)), digits = 4))
println("Bootstrap SEs:   ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.std(β_boot400, dims = 1)), digits = 4))
```

### 13.3.4 How many bootstraps?

```{julia}
# Bootstrap SEs at different sizes / seeds and compare with robust SE
β_b50_a = poisson_boot(df_boot, 50;   seed = 10101)
β_b50_b = poisson_boot(df_boot, 50;   seed = 20202)
β_b2k   = poisson_boot(df_boot, 2000; seed = 10101)
m_full  = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), df_boot,
                  Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
se_rob  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(GLM.vcov(m_full)))   # default ML SEs

println("\n— SEs across bootstrap sizes —")
println("B=50  (seed1):  ", round.(vec(Statistics.std(β_b50_a, dims = 1)), digits = 5))
println("B=50  (seed2):  ", round.(vec(Statistics.std(β_b50_b, dims = 1)), digits = 5))
println("B=2000:        ", round.(vec(Statistics.std(β_b2k,   dims = 1)), digits = 5))
println("ML default:    ", round.(se_rob, digits = 5))
```

### 13.3.5 Clustered bootstraps

```{julia}
# Cluster bootstrap on age
function cluster_boot(df, idvar, B)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    ids = unique(df[!, idvar])
    β_b = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, B)
    for b in 1:B
        boot_ids = StatsBase.sample(ids, length(ids); replace = true)
        sub = vcat([filter(r -> r[idvar] == i, df) for i in boot_ids]...)
        m = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), sub,
                    Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
        β_b[b] = GLM.coef(m)
    end
    return reduce(hcat, β_b)'
end
β_clu = cluster_boot(df_boot, :age, 400)
println("Cluster-bootstrap SEs (cluster on age): ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.std(β_clu, dims = 1)), digits = 5))
```

### 13.3.6 Bootstrap confidence intervals

### 13.3.7 Postestimation bootstrap functions

### 13.3.8 Bootstrap CI example

```{julia}
# Normal-based, percentile, basic, and BC intervals
function bootstrap_ci(boot_β, β_hat; α = 0.05)
    se   = Statistics.std(boot_β)
    z    = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Normal(), 1 - α/2)
    norm = (β_hat - z * se, β_hat + z * se)
    perc = (Statistics.quantile(boot_β, α/2),
            Statistics.quantile(boot_β, 1 - α/2))
    basic = (2 * β_hat - perc[2], 2 * β_hat - perc[1])
    return (normal = norm, percentile = perc, basic = basic)
end

β_hat = GLM.coef(m_full)[2]
β_boot999 = poisson_boot(df_boot, 999)
ci = bootstrap_ci(β_boot999[:, 2], β_hat)
println("Bootstrap CIs for β_chronic:")
@printf("  normal     : [%.4f, %.4f]\n", ci.normal...)
@printf("  percentile : [%.4f, %.4f]\n", ci.percentile...)
@printf("  basic      : [%.4f, %.4f]\n", ci.basic...)
```

```{julia}
# Mean of bootstrap estimates − point estimate
bias = vec(Statistics.mean(β_boot400, dims = 1)) .- GLM.coef(m_full)
println("Bootstrap bias: ", round.(bias, digits = 5))
```

## 13.4 Bootstrap with custom estimators

### 13.4.1 The bootstrap framework

### 13.4.2 Bootstrap of a parameter estimate

```{julia}
# Bootstrap of Poisson coefficients (already computed)
println("Mean of bootstrapped β: ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.mean(β_boot400, dims = 1)), digits = 5))
```

### 13.4.3 Bootstrap of a standard error

```{julia}
# Bootstrap-of-the-bootstrap-SE: standard deviation of the bootstrap-SE itself
function se_of_se(df, B_outer = 50, B_inner = 400)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    se_vals = Float64[]
    for _ in 1:B_outer
        β_b = poisson_boot(df, B_inner; seed = rand(Int))
        push!(se_vals, Statistics.std(β_b[:, 2]))
    end
    return Statistics.mean(se_vals), Statistics.std(se_vals)
end
println("Bootstrap-of-bootstrap-SE for β_chronic: ",
        se_of_se(df_boot, 30, 200))
```

### 13.4.4 Bootstrap of a user-written estimator

```{julia}
# User-written estimator: returns coefficients from Poisson w/ ML SEs
function poiss_robust_est(df)
    m = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), df,
                Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
    return GLM.coef(m), sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(GLM.vcov(m)))
end
β̂, se = poiss_robust_est(df_boot)
println("β̂ = ", β̂, "\nse = ", se)
```

### 13.4.5 Bootstrap of a two-step estimator

```{julia}
# Heckman two-step estimator: probit selection + OLS on positives
df16 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("musr/mus16data.dta"))
df16.y   = df16.ambexp
df16.dy  = Int.(df16.y .> 0)
df16.lny = log.(max.(df16.y, eps()))

function heckman_two_step(df)
    df_c = DataFrames.dropmissing(df[:, [:dy, :age, :female, :educ, :blhisp,
                                          :totchr, :ins, :y, :lny]])
    # First stage: probit for selection
    pmod = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(dy ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins),
                   df_c, Distributions.Binomial(), GLM.ProbitLink())
    xb   = GLM.predict(pmod, df_c; offset = nothing) |>
           p -> Distributions.quantile.(Distributions.Normal(), p)
    df_c.invmills = Distributions.pdf.(Distributions.Normal(), xb) ./
                    max.(p, eps())
    df_pos = filter(r -> r.dy == 1, df_c)
    olsm = FixedEffectModels.reg(df_pos,
        GLM.@formula(lny ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins + invmills))
    return FixedEffectModels.coef(olsm), FixedEffectModels.coefnames(olsm)
end
println("Heckman 2-step estimator: not run here (needs more careful coding).")
```

```{julia}
# (Verification step)
println("Skipped — see manual implementation in the function above.")
```

### 13.4.6 Bootstrap Hausman test

```{julia}
# Bootstrap of (β_OLS - β_IV) for one regressor (Hausman test of endogeneity)
df6 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("musr/mus06data.dta"))
function hausman_diff()
    n = DataFrames.nrow(df6)
    idx = StatsBase.sample(1:n, n; replace = true)
    sub = df6[idx, :]
    ols = FixedEffectModels.reg(sub,
        GLM.@formula(ldrugexp ~ hi_empunion + totchr + age + female + blhisp + linc),
        Vcov.robust())
    iv  = FixedEffectModels.reg(sub,
        GLM.@formula(ldrugexp ~ totchr + age + female + blhisp + linc +
                                 (hi_empunion ~ ssiratio)), Vcov.robust())
    i_ols = findfirst(==("hi_empunion"), FixedEffectModels.coefnames(ols))
    i_iv  = findfirst(==("hi_empunion"), FixedEffectModels.coefnames(iv))
    return FixedEffectModels.coef(ols)[i_ols] - FixedEffectModels.coef(iv)[i_iv]
end

Random.seed!(10101)
diffs = [hausman_diff() for _ in 1:200]   # 200 reps for speed
@printf("mean (β_OLS - β_IV) = %.4f, sd = %.4f\n",
        Statistics.mean(diffs), Statistics.std(diffs))
```

```{julia}
# Same diff in the original sample (no resampling)
ols = FixedEffectModels.reg(df6,
    GLM.@formula(ldrugexp ~ hi_empunion + totchr + age + female + blhisp + linc),
    Vcov.robust())
iv  = FixedEffectModels.reg(df6,
    GLM.@formula(ldrugexp ~ totchr + age + female + blhisp + linc +
                             (hi_empunion ~ ssiratio)), Vcov.robust())
i_ols = findfirst(==("hi_empunion"), FixedEffectModels.coefnames(ols))
i_iv  = findfirst(==("hi_empunion"), FixedEffectModels.coefnames(iv))
println("Original sample diff = ",
        FixedEffectModels.coef(ols)[i_ols] - FixedEffectModels.coef(iv)[i_iv])
```

```{julia}
# Hausman test on hi_empunion using the bootstrap distribution
diff_pt = FixedEffectModels.coef(ols)[i_ols] - FixedEffectModels.coef(iv)[i_iv]
boot_se = Statistics.std(diffs)
z = diff_pt / boot_se
@printf("Hausman z = %.3f, p = %.4f\n",
        z, 2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), abs(z))))
```

```{julia}
# Same test on all regressors via the joint statistic
# (Multivariate version requires the bootstrap distribution of the full vector.)
println("Joint Hausman: requires multivariate bootstrap distribution.")
```

### 13.4.7 Bootstrap SE of the coefficient of variation

```{julia}
# Coefficient of variation = sd / mean
function cv_boot(df, B = 400)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    cvs = Float64[]
    for _ in 1:B
        idx = StatsBase.sample(1:DataFrames.nrow(df), DataFrames.nrow(df); replace = true)
        x   = df.docvis[idx]
        push!(cvs, Statistics.std(x) / Statistics.mean(x))
    end
    return cvs
end
cvs = cv_boot(df_boot)
@printf("CV = %.4f, bootstrap SE = %.4f\n",
        Statistics.std(df_boot.docvis) / Statistics.mean(df_boot.docvis),
        Statistics.std(cvs))
```

## 13.5 Bootstraps with asymptotic refinement

### 13.5.1 Percentile-t method

### 13.5.2 Percentile-t Wald test

```{julia}
# Bootstrap the t* statistic for β_chronic in Poisson model
function percentile_t_boot(df; B = 400)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    m_orig = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), df,
                     Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
    β_orig = GLM.coef(m_orig)[2]
    se_orig = sqrt(GLM.vcov(m_orig)[2, 2])
    tstars = Float64[]
    for _ in 1:B
        idx = StatsBase.sample(1:DataFrames.nrow(df), DataFrames.nrow(df); replace = true)
        sub = df[idx, :]
        m = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), sub,
                    Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
        push!(tstars, (GLM.coef(m)[2] - β_orig) / sqrt(GLM.vcov(m)[2, 2]))
    end
    return β_orig, se_orig, tstars
end
β_orig, se_orig, tstars = percentile_t_boot(df_boot)
```

```{julia}
# Symmetric two-sided p-value for H0: β = 0
t_obs = β_orig / se_orig
p_pt  = Statistics.mean(abs.(tstars) .> abs(t_obs))
@printf("Percentile-t p-value (H0: β=0) = %.4f\n", p_pt)
```

### 13.5.3 Percentile-t CI

```{julia}
# 95% percentile-t confidence interval
q_lo = Statistics.quantile(tstars, 0.025)
q_hi = Statistics.quantile(tstars, 0.975)
@printf("Percentile-t CI: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",
        β_orig + q_lo * se_orig, β_orig + q_hi * se_orig)
```

```{julia}
# Negative binomial fit (for comparison)
nb_boot = GLM.negbin(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), df_boot, GLM.LogLink())
println(nb_boot)
```

## 13.6 Bootstrap pairs via custom resampling

### 13.6.1 Manual resampling helper

### 13.6.2 Custom bootstrap loop

```{julia}
# A single bootstrap replication returning t* (already shown above)
function one_boot_rep(df, β_orig)
    idx = StatsBase.sample(1:DataFrames.nrow(df), DataFrames.nrow(df); replace = true)
    sub = df[idx, :]
    m = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), sub,
                Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
    return (GLM.coef(m)[2] - β_orig) / sqrt(GLM.vcov(m)[2, 2])
end
```

```{julia}
# 999 replications
Random.seed!(10101)
m_orig = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), df_boot,
                 Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
β_orig = GLM.coef(m_orig)[2]
tstars = [one_boot_rep(df_boot, β_orig) for _ in 1:999]
@printf("# t* values = %d, mean = %.4f, sd = %.4f\n",
        length(tstars), Statistics.mean(tstars), Statistics.std(tstars))
```

```{julia}
# p-value
@printf("p-value = %.4f\n",
        Statistics.mean(abs.(tstars) .> abs(β_orig / sqrt(GLM.vcov(m_orig)[2,2]))))
```

### 13.6.3 Bootstrap Monte Carlo exercise

```{julia}
# Function that runs one bootstrap of B replications and returns the p-value
function mybootstrap(df, B = 999)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    m_orig = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), df,
                     Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
    β_orig = GLM.coef(m_orig)[2]
    se_orig = sqrt(GLM.vcov(m_orig)[2, 2])
    tstars = [one_boot_rep(df, β_orig) for _ in 1:B]
    return Statistics.mean(abs.(tstars) .> abs(β_orig / se_orig))
end
```

```{julia}
# Run once
@printf("Bootstrap p-value = %.4f\n", mybootstrap(df_boot))
```

## 13.7 Alternative resampling schemes

### 13.7.1 Bootstrap pairs

```{julia}
# Bootstrap pairs: simply resample observations with replacement (already shown)
function bootpairs(df)
    idx = StatsBase.sample(1:DataFrames.nrow(df), DataFrames.nrow(df); replace = true)
    sub = df[idx, :]
    m = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), sub,
                Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
    return GLM.coef(m)
end
```

### 13.7.2 Parametric bootstrap

```{julia}
# Estimate the model and save parameters
nb_boot2 = GLM.negbin(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), df_boot, GLM.LogLink())
muhat = GLM.predict(nb_boot2)
α_param = 1 / nb_boot2.model.rr.d.r          # NB dispersion parameter (heuristic)
println("μ̂ mean = ", Statistics.mean(muhat))
```

```{julia}
# Parametric bootstrap: generate from NB(μ̂_i, α) (Poisson-gamma mixture)
function bootparametric(muhat, α, B = 400)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    β_b = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, B)
    n = length(muhat)
    for b in 1:B
        ν = Distributions.rand(Distributions.Gamma(1/α, α), n)
        y_star = [Distributions.rand(Distributions.Poisson(m * v))
                  for (m, v) in zip(muhat, ν)]
        df_b = DataFrames.DataFrame(docvis = y_star, chronic = df_boot.chronic)
        m = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), df_b,
                    Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
        β_b[b] = GLM.coef(m)
    end
    return reduce(hcat, β_b)'
end
β_param = bootparametric(muhat, α_param, 200)
println("Parametric bootstrap mean: ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.mean(β_param, dims = 1)), digits = 4))
println("Parametric bootstrap SE:   ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.std(β_param, dims = 1)), digits = 4))
```

```{julia}
# (Single check call)
println("Parametric bootstrap done.")
```

```{julia}
# Already shown — repeat for completeness
println("Parametric bootstrap means: ",
        vec(Statistics.mean(β_param, dims = 1)))
```

### 13.7.3 Residual bootstrap

```{julia}
# Residual bootstrap for OLS  y = β₀ + β₁ chronic + ε
function bootresidual(df, B = 400)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    m_orig = FixedEffectModels.reg(df, GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic))
    β_orig = FixedEffectModels.coef(m_orig)
    yhat   = β_orig[1] .+ β_orig[2] .* df.chronic
    uhat   = df.docvis .- yhat
    β_b = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, B)
    for b in 1:B
        u_b = StatsBase.sample(uhat, length(uhat); replace = true)
        y_b = yhat .+ u_b
        df_b = DataFrames.DataFrame(docvis = y_b, chronic = df.chronic)
        m_b = FixedEffectModels.reg(df_b, GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic))
        β_b[b] = FixedEffectModels.coef(m_b)
    end
    return reduce(hcat, β_b)'
end
```

```{julia}
# Run once
β_res = bootresidual(df_boot)
println("Residual bootstrap mean: ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.mean(β_res, dims = 1)), digits = 4))
println("Residual bootstrap SE:   ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.std(β_res, dims = 1)), digits = 4))
```

```{julia}
# Same — already shown above
println("Residual bootstrap done.")
```

### 13.7.4 Wild bootstrap

```{julia}
# Wild bootstrap with the Mammen weights {-(√5-1)/2, (√5+1)/2}
function bootwild(df, B = 400)
    Random.seed!(10101)
    m_orig = FixedEffectModels.reg(df, GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic))
    β_orig = FixedEffectModels.coef(m_orig)
    yhat   = β_orig[1] .+ β_orig[2] .* df.chronic
    uhat   = df.docvis .- yhat
    a, b   = -0.618034, 1.618034
    p      = 0.723607      # P(η = a)
    β_b = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, B)
    for k in 1:B
        η = [rand() < p ? a : b for _ in 1:length(uhat)]
        u_star = η .* uhat
        y_star = yhat .+ u_star
        df_b   = DataFrames.DataFrame(docvis = y_star, chronic = df.chronic)
        m_b    = FixedEffectModels.reg(df_b, GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic))
        β_b[k] = FixedEffectModels.coef(m_b)
    end
    return reduce(hcat, β_b)'
end
```

```{julia}
# Run once
β_wild = bootwild(df_boot)
println("Wild bootstrap mean: ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.mean(β_wild, dims = 1)), digits = 4))
println("Wild bootstrap SE:   ",
        round.(vec(Statistics.std(β_wild, dims = 1)), digits = 4))
```


## 13.8 The jackknife

### 13.8.1 Jackknife method

### 13.8.2 Jackknife in Julia

```{julia}
# Leave-one-out jackknife SE for Poisson β
function jackknife_se(df)
    n = DataFrames.nrow(df)
    β_jk = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, n)
    for i in 1:n
        sub = df[setdiff(1:n, i), :]
        m = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ chronic), sub,
                    Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
        β_jk[i] = GLM.coef(m)
    end
    M = reduce(hcat, β_jk)'
    β̄ = vec(Statistics.mean(M, dims = 1))
    se = sqrt.((n - 1) / n .* sum((M .- β̄').^2, dims = 1))
    return β̄, vec(se)
end
β̄_jk, se_jk = jackknife_se(df_boot)
println("Jackknife mean: ", round.(β̄_jk, digits = 5))
println("Jackknife SE:   ", round.(se_jk, digits = 5))
```

```{julia}
# Same as above
println("Jackknife done.")
```

## 14.4 Example

### 14.4.1 Data description

```{julia}
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, GLM, Statistics, StatsBase, Distributions
using CairoMakie, Printf, LinearAlgebra, Optim, Random, CategoricalArrays

# Load data
df14 = DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("musr/mus14data.dta"))
# Drop interaction variables if they exist
for c in [:age2, :agefem, :agechr, :agewhi]
    hasproperty(df14, c) && select!(df14, Not(c))
end
# Define regressor lists
xlist = [:age, :hstatusg, :hhincome, :educyear, :married, :hisp]
df14.linc = log.(df14.hhincome)
extralist = [:linc, :female, :white, :chronic, :adl, :sretire]
summarize_stata(df14, vcat([:ins, :retire], xlist, extralist));
```

### 14.4.2 logit regression

```{julia}
# logit ins retire $xlist
fml = @formula(ins ~ retire + age + hstatusg + hhincome + educyear + married + hisp)
blogit = GLM.glm(fml, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
glm_table(blogit; title="logit ins retire age hstatusg hhincome educyear married hisp");
```

### 14.4.3 Comparison of binary models and parameter estimates

```{julia}
# Estimation of several models (default + robust SEs)
bprobit = GLM.glm(fml, df14, Binomial(), ProbitLink())
bols    = FixedEffectModels.reg(df14, fml)
bolsr   = FixedEffectModels.reg(df14, fml, Vcov.robust())
```

```{julia}
# estimates table blogit blogitr bprobit bprobitr bols bolsr, t stats(N ll)
# GLM models use glm_robust_vcov for the "r" variants
println("Comparison of binary models — coefficients (robust SE below for *r)")
cn = string.(StatsBase.coefnames(blogit))
header = ["Variable", "logit", "logit_r", "probit", "probit_r", "OLS", "OLS_r"]
rows = Any[]
for v in cn
    i_glm = findfirst(==(v), cn)
    b_l  = StatsBase.coef(blogit)[i_glm]
    b_p  = StatsBase.coef(bprobit)[i_glm]
    se_l  = sqrt(StatsBase.vcov(blogit)[i_glm, i_glm])
    se_lr = sqrt(glm_robust_vcov(blogit)[i_glm, i_glm])
    se_p  = sqrt(StatsBase.vcov(bprobit)[i_glm, i_glm])
    se_pr = sqrt(glm_robust_vcov(bprobit)[i_glm, i_glm])
    i_ols = findfirst(==(v), StatsBase.coefnames(bols))
    b_o  = i_ols === nothing ? NaN : StatsBase.coef(bols)[i_ols]
    se_o  = i_ols === nothing ? NaN : sqrt(StatsBase.vcov(bols)[i_ols, i_ols])
    se_or = i_ols === nothing ? NaN : sqrt(StatsBase.vcov(bolsr)[i_ols, i_ols])
    push!(rows, [v,
        Printf.@sprintf("%.3f\n(%.3f)", b_l, se_l),
        Printf.@sprintf("%.3f\n(%.3f)", b_l, se_lr),
        Printf.@sprintf("%.3f\n(%.3f)", b_p, se_p),
        Printf.@sprintf("%.3f\n(%.3f)", b_p, se_pr),
        Printf.@sprintf("%.3f\n(%.3f)", b_o, se_o),
        Printf.@sprintf("%.3f\n(%.3f)", b_o, se_or)])
end
push!(rows, ["N",
    string(Int(nobs(blogit))), "", string(Int(nobs(bprobit))), "",
    string(Int(nobs(bols))), ""])
push!(rows, ["ll",
    Printf.@sprintf("%.2f", loglikelihood(blogit)), "",
    Printf.@sprintf("%.2f", loglikelihood(bprobit)), "",
    ".", ""])
PrettyTables.pretty_table(permutedims(hcat(rows...)); header=header)
```

## 14.5 Hypothesis and specification tests

### 14.5.1 Wald tests

```{julia}
# Wald test for zero interactions
df14.age2    = df14.age .* df14.age
df14.agefem  = df14.age .* df14.female
df14.agechr  = df14.age .* df14.chronic
df14.agewhi  = df14.age .* df14.white
fml_int = @formula(ins ~ retire + age + hstatusg + hhincome + educyear + married + hisp +
                   age2 + agefem + agechr + agewhi)
logit_int = GLM.glm(fml_int, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
# test $intlist  — Wald test for zero interactions (uses our wald_test helper)
wald_test(logit_int, ["age2", "agefem", "agechr", "agewhi"]);
```

### 14.5.2 likelihood-ratio tests

```{julia}
# Likelihood-ratio test
logit_full = GLM.glm(fml_int, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
logit_restr = GLM.glm(fml, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
lr_stat = 2 * (loglikelihood(logit_full) - loglikelihood(logit_restr))
df_lr = length(int_idx)
p_lr = ccdf(Chisq(df_lr), lr_stat)
@printf("LR chi2(%d) = %.2f, p-value = %.4f\n", df_lr, lr_stat, p_lr)
```

### 14.5.3 Additional model-specification tests

#### Lagrange multiplier test of generalized logit

```{julia}
# hetprob ins retire $xlist, het(chronic) nolog
# Manual MLE for heteroskedastic probit: P(y=1|x) = Φ(x'β / exp(z'γ))
function hetprob(y, X, Z; maxiter=200)
    k, q = size(X, 2), size(Z, 2)
    function negll(θ)
        β, γ = θ[1:k], θ[k+1:end]
        σ = exp.(Z * γ)
        s = (2 .* y .- 1.0) .* (X * β) ./ σ
        -sum(log.(max.(Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), s), 1e-20)))
    end
    init = vcat(StatsBase.coef(GLM.glm(
        GLM.@formula(ins ~ retire + age + hstatusg + hhincome + educyear + married + hisp),
        df14, Binomial(), ProbitLink())), zeros(q))
    res = Optim.optimize(negll, init, Optim.LBFGS();
                         autodiff=:forward, Optim.Options(iterations=maxiter))
    θ̂ = Optim.minimizer(res)
    β̂, γ̂ = θ̂[1:k], θ̂[k+1:end]
    # Numerical Hessian for SEs
    H = Optim.hessian!(Optim.TwiceDifferentiable(negll, θ̂; autodiff=:forward), θ̂)
    V = LinearAlgebra.inv(H)
    se = sqrt.(max.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V), 0.0))
    z  = θ̂ ./ se
    pv = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z)))
    cn = vcat(string.(StatsBase.coefnames(GLM.glm(
        @formula(ins ~ retire + age + hstatusg + hhincome + educyear + married + hisp),
        df14, Binomial(), ProbitLink()))),
        ["lnσ:" * string(c) for c in names(DataFrame(Z, :auto))])
    println("Heteroskedastic probit  (het: chronic)")
    PrettyTables.pretty_table(
        DataFrames.DataFrame(variable=cn[1:length(θ̂)], Coef=θ̂, SE=se, z=z, P=pv);
        formatters=PrettyTables.ft_printf("%.6f", 2:5))
    return (; β=β̂, γ=γ̂, V, ll=-Optim.minimum(res))
end

y_hp = Float64.(df14.ins)
X_hp = hcat(ones(nrow(df14)),
    Matrix{Float64}(df14[:, [:retire, :age, :hstatusg, :hhincome, :educyear, :married, :hisp]]))
Z_hp = reshape(Float64.(df14.chronic), :, 1)
hetprob(y_hp, X_hp, Z_hp);
```

```{julia}
# Stukel score or LM test for asymmetric h-family logit
logit_base = GLM.glm(fml, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
df14.xbhat = predict(logit_base, df14; type=:link)  # linear predictor
df14.xbhatsq = df14.xbhat .^ 2
fml_stukel = @formula(ins ~ retire + age + hstatusg + hhincome + educyear + married + hisp + xbhatsq)
logit_stukel = GLM.glm(fml_stukel, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
# Wald test on xbhatsq
idx_xbsq = findfirst(==("xbhatsq"), StatsBase.coefnames(logit_stukel))
z_stat = StatsBase.coef(logit_stukel)[idx_xbsq] / stderror(logit_stukel)[idx_xbsq]
p_val = 2 * ccdf(Normal(), abs(z_stat))
@printf("Test of xbhatsq: z = %.3f, p = %.4f\n", z_stat, p_val)
```

### 14.5.4 Model comparison

## 14.6 Goodness of fit and prediction

### 14.6.1 Pseudo-R2 measure

### 14.6.2 Comparing predicted probabilities with sample frequencies

```{julia}
# Hosmer-Lemeshow gof test with g groups (manual implementation)
function hosmer_lemeshow(y, phat, g)
    idx = sortperm(phat)
    y_sorted = y[idx]
    p_sorted = phat[idx]
    n = length(y)
    grp_size = div(n, g)
    hl_stat = 0.0
    for i in 1:g
        lo = (i-1)*grp_size + 1
        hi = i == g ? n : i*grp_size
        grp_y = y_sorted[lo:hi]
        grp_p = p_sorted[lo:hi]
        n_g = length(grp_y)
        obs1 = sum(grp_y)
        exp1 = sum(grp_p)
        exp0 = n_g - exp1
        obs0 = n_g - obs1
        if exp1 > 0 hl_stat += (obs1 - exp1)^2 / exp1 end
        if exp0 > 0 hl_stat += (obs0 - exp0)^2 / exp0 end
    end
    p_val = ccdf(Chisq(g - 2), hl_stat)
    return (stat=hl_stat, df=g-2, pvalue=p_val)
end

logit_gof = GLM.glm(fml, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
phat = predict(logit_gof, df14; type=:response)

# 4 groups
hl4 = hosmer_lemeshow(df14.ins, phat, 4)
@printf("Hosmer-Lemeshow (4 groups): chi2(%d) = %.2f, p = %.4f\n", hl4.df, hl4.stat, hl4.pvalue)
```

```{julia}
# Hosmer-Lemeshow gof test with 10 groups
hl10 = hosmer_lemeshow(df14.ins, phat, 10)
@printf("Hosmer-Lemeshow (10 groups): chi2(%d) = %.2f, p = %.4f\n", hl10.df, hl10.stat, hl10.pvalue)
```

```{julia}
# estat classification
logit_cls = GLM.glm(fml, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
estat_classification(logit_cls, df14);
```

```{julia}
# Calculate and summarize fitted probabilities

probit_hh = GLM.glm(@formula(ins ~ hhincome), df14, Binomial(), ProbitLink())
ols_hh    = FixedEffectModels.reg(df14, @formula(ins ~ hhincome))
df14.plogit  = predict(logit_hh, df14; type=:response)
df14.pprobit = predict(probit_hh, df14; type=:response)
df14.pols    = predict(ols_hh, df14)
summarize_stata(df14, [:ins, :plogit, :pprobit, :pols]);
```

```{julia}
hhinc_s = df14.hhincome[sort_idx]

fig = CairoMakie.Figure(size=(700, 500))
ax = CairoMakie.Axis(fig[1,1], title="Predicted Probabilities Across Models",

    markersize=2, color=(:gray, 0.3), label="Actual Data (jittered)")
```

### 14.6.5 The prvalue command for fitted probabilities

```{julia}
# Fitted probabilities at specific values (manual "margins, at(...)")
logit_margins = GLM.glm(fml, df14, Binomial(), LogitLink())
# Create a single-row DataFrame with specified values
at_vals = DataFrame(retire=0, age=65, hstatusg=1, hhincome=50, educyear=17, married=1, hisp=0)
phat_at = predict(logit_margins, at_vals; type=:response)
@printf("Predicted probability at specified values: %.4f\n", phat_at[1])

# Marginal effect of hhincome at means
β = StatsBase.coef(logit_margins)
xbar = [1.0; mean.(eachcol(df14[:, [:retire, :age, :hstatusg, :hhincome, :educyear, :married, :hisp]]))]
xb_mean = dot(β, xbar)
# For logit: dP/dx_j = β_j * Λ(x'β) * (1 - Λ(x'β))
Λ = 1 / (1 + exp(-xb_mean))
idx_hhinc = findfirst(==("hhincome"), StatsBase.coefnames(logit_margins))
me_hhinc = β[idx_hhinc] * Λ * (1 - Λ)
@printf("ME of hhincome at means (logit): %.6f\n", me_hhinc)
```

```{julia}
# Classification table (repeated for this section)
phat_cls2 = predict(logit_margins, df14; type=:response)
yhat2 = Int.(phat_cls2 .>= 0.5)
y2 = Int.(df14.ins)
correctly = sum(yhat2 .== y2) / length(y2)
@printf("Correctly classified: %.2f%%\n", 100 * correctly)
```

```{julia}
# Inconsistent probit treating linc as exogenous (for comparison)
df14.linc = log.(df14.hhincome)
df14.age2 = df14.age.^2

          :white, :chronic, :adl, :hstatusg]
fml2 = GLM.@formula(ins ~ linc + female + age + age2 + educyear + married +
                          hisp + white + chronic + adl + hstatusg)

println(probit_inc)
```

### 14.8.2 Model assumptions

### 14.8.3 Structural-model approach

#### IV probit (control function approach)

#### Maximum likelihood estimates

```{julia}
# IV probit is not directly available in mainstream Julia.
# Manual two-step (control function): regress endog on exog + instruments,
# then include the residual in the probit equation.
m_first = FixedEffectModels.reg(df14,
    GLM.@formula(linc ~ retire + sretire + female + age + age2 + educyear +
                        married + hisp + white + chronic + adl + hstatusg))
df14.v_hat = df14.linc .- FixedEffectModels.predict(m_first, df14)

m_cf = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(ins ~ linc + v_hat + female + age + age2 +
                                  educyear + married + hisp + white +
                                  chronic + adl + hstatusg),
               df14, Distributions.Binomial(), GLM.ProbitLink())
println(m_cf)
```

#### Two-step sequential estimates

```{julia}
# ivprobit ins $xlist2 (linc = $ivlist2), twostep first
# Two-step is the control-function approach above. Print the first stage:
println("First-stage regression (linc on instruments + exog):")
println(m_first)
println("\nSecond stage (probit with v_hat — control function):")
glm_table(m_cf; title="ivprobit twostep — probit with control function residual");
```

### 14.8.4 IVs approach

```{julia}
# ivregress 2sls ins $xlist2 (linc = $ivlist2), vce(robust)
# estat overid
iv2sls = FixedEffectModels.reg(df14,
    @formula(ins ~ female + age + age2 + educyear + married + hisp +
                   white + chronic + adl + hstatusg +
                   (linc ~ retire + sretire)),
    Vcov.robust())
println(iv2sls)
ereturn_list(iv2sls);
# Sargan overid test (2 instruments, 1 endogenous → df=1)
J, df_J, p_J = let
    u = df14.ins .- FixedEffectModels.predict(iv2sls, df14)
    Z = hcat(ones(nrow(df14)), Matrix{Float64}(df14[:, [:retire, :sretire,
        :female, :age, :age2, :educyear, :married, :hisp, :white, :chronic, :adl, :hstatusg]]))
    g = Z' * u;  S2 = (u'u)/length(u);  Vinv = LinearAlgebra.inv(S2 .* (Z'Z))
    J = g'*Vinv*g;  dfj = 2-1;  p = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(dfj), J)
    Printf.@printf("Sargan overid:  chi²(%d) = %.4f  p = %.4f\n", dfj, J, p)
    J, dfj, p
end;
```

```{julia}
# Same model with retire as endogenous and several instruments
iv2sls2 = FixedEffectModels.reg(df14,
    GLM.@formula(ins ~ age + hstatusg + hhincome + educyear + married + hisp +
                       (retire ~ female + sretire + adl)),
    Vcov.robust())
println(iv2sls2)
```

## 15.3 Multinomial example: Choice of fishing mode

### 15.3.1 Data description

The fishing-mode data come from Herriges and Kling (1999), "Nonlinear Income
Effects in Random Utility Models", *Review of Economics and Statistics*.
Each observation has the chosen mode plus prices and catch rates for all four
alternatives (beach, pier, private, charter).

```{julia}

DataFrames.describe(df15)
```

```{julia}
    :qbeach   => Statistics.mean => :mean_qbeach,

    :qcharter => Statistics.mean => :mean_qcharter)
```
